Pittsburgh Steelers:
{{Short description|none}} {{Use mdy dates|date=June 2024}} The [[Pittsburgh Steelers]] franchise has had 17 head coaches throughout its history. Founded as the Pittsburgh Pirates in [[1933 Pittsburgh Pirates (NFL) season|1933]],{{cite web|url= http://www.nfl.com/history/chronology/1931-1940#1933|title= NFL history 1933|website= NFL.com|access-date= April 27, 2008|archive-date= May 10, 2020|archive-url= https://web.archive.org/web/20200510223235/http://www.nfl.com/history/chronology/1931-1940#1933|url-status= live}} the name was changed to the Steelers prior to the [[1940 Pittsburgh Steelers season|1940]] season to celebrate the city's heritage of producing [[Steel mill|steel]].{{cite web|url=http://news.steelers.com/MediaContent/2007/08/22/05/Steelers_History_80311.pdf |title=Steelers' History |access-date=April 27, 2008 |publisher=Pittsburgh Steelers |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20080528074040/http://news.steelers.com/MediaContent/2007/08/22/05/Steelers_History_80311.pdf |archive-date=May 28, 2008 }} [[Joe Bach]] served two separate terms as head coach and [[Walt Kiesling]] served three separate terms. During the {{nfly|1943}} and {{nfly|1944}} seasons, due to the number of [[History of American football#War years|players who fought]] in [[World War II]], the Steelers combined their team with [[Steagles|Philadelphia]] and [[Card-Pitt|Chicago]], respectively. During these seasons, Kiesling shared coaching duties with [[Greasy Neale]] and [[Phil Handler]],{{cite web|url= http://www.profootballhof.com/hof/member.jsp?player_id=114|title= Walt Kiesling|access-date= May 5, 2008|publisher= ProFootballhof.com|archive-date= May 5, 2008|archive-url= https://web.archive.org/web/20080505054645/http://www.profootballhof.com/hof/member.jsp?player_id=114|url-status= live}} who have not been included within this list. Since the beginning of the Super Bowl era in 1970, the Steelers have had only four head coaches, the fewest of any NFL franchise. Struggling with a losing record for much of the franchise's early years, the team's first season with more wins than losses was coached by [[Jock Sutherland]] in [[1942 Pittsburgh Steelers season|1942]]. In [[1947 Pittsburgh Steelers season|1947]], under Sutherland, the Steelers played their first playoff game against the [[Philadelphia Eagles]]. Ten of the 17 head coaches spent their entire professional coaching careers with the franchise, including Kiesling, [[Johnny Blood]], and [[Chuck Noll]], who have also been voted into the [[Pro Football Hall of Fame]].{{cite web|url=http://www.profootballhof.com/hof/teams.html |title=Hall of Famers by Franchise |access-date=April 27, 2008 |publisher=ProFootballHOF.com |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20080412001112/http://www.profootballhof.com/hof/teams.html |archive-date=April 12, 2008 |url-status=dead }} One of only four men to coach the same team for 23 years, Noll retired in [[1991 Pittsburgh Steelers season|1991]]. [[Bill Cowher]], who was Noll's replacement, coached the Steelers to their fifth Super Bowl victory in [[Super Bowl XL]] (2005) and was voted into the Hall of Fame in 2020. The Steelers' sixth Super Bowl win came in [[Super Bowl XLIII]] (2008) under head coach [[Mike Tomlin]], who was hired to replace the retiring Cowher in 2007.{{cite press release|title=Steelers Name Mike Tomlin the 16th Head Coach in Team History. |url=http://news.steelers.com/article/73526/|publisher=Pittsburgh Steelers|date=January 23, 2007|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20070125160843/http://news.steelers.com/article/73526/|archive-date=January 25, 2007 |access-date=July 14, 2016}}{{cite news|first= Gerry|last= Dulac|title= Tomlin, 34, is NFL's rising coaching star|url= http://www.post-gazette.com/pg/07018/754788-66.stm|newspaper= [[Pittsburgh Post-Gazette]]|date= January 18, 2007|access-date= April 27, 2008|archive-date= May 1, 2007|archive-url= https://web.archive.org/web/20070501125446/http://www.post-gazette.com/pg/07018/754788-66.stm|url-status= live}} ==Key== {| class="wikitable" style="font-size:90%;" |- |'''#''' |Number of coachesA running total of the number of coaches of the Steelers. New England Patriots:
{{Short description|None}} {{Use mdy dates|date=June 2024}} [[File:Mike Vrabel 2023.png|thumb|235x235px|[[Mike Vrabel]], the current head coach of the Patriots, also played for the team as a [[linebacker]] from 2001 to 2008.]] The [[New England Patriots]] are a professional [[American football]] team based in [[Foxborough, Massachusetts]]. They are a member of the [[AFC East|East Division]] of the [[American Football Conference]] (AFC) in the [[National Football League]] (NFL). The team began as the Boston Patriots in the [[American Football League]], a league which [[AFL-NFL merger|merged]] with the [[National Football League]] before the [[1970 NFL season|1970 season]].{{cite web |url=http://www.profootballhof.com/history/team.jsp?franchise_id=19 |title=Boston Patriots |access-date=May 13, 2008 |publisher=Pro Football Hall of Fame |archive-date=June 4, 2008 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20080604135922/http://www.profootballhof.com/history/team.jsp?FRANCHISE_ID=19 |url-status=live }} There have been 16 head coaches for the Patriots franchise. [[Lou Saban]] became the first coach of the Patriots in 1960, although he was fired partway through their second season.{{cite web |url=http://www.profootballhof.com/history/team.jsp?FRANCHISE_ID=4 |title=Buffalo Bills |access-date=May 13, 2008 |publisher=Pro Football Hall of Fame |archive-date=June 4, 2008 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20080604135926/http://www.profootballhof.com/history/team.jsp?FRANCHISE_ID=4 |url-status=live }} The 16th and current head coach is [[Mike Vrabel]], who was hired on January 12, 2025. He is the second coach in the club's history to have played for the team (2001–2008){{Cite web |last=Goss |first=Nick |date=2025-01-12 |title=Patriots hire Mike Vrabel as next head coach, completing brief search |url=https://www.nbcsportsboston.com/nfl/new-england-patriots/mike-vrabel-hired-head-coach-jerod-mayo/679964/ |access-date=2025-01-12 |website=NBC Sports Boston |language=en-US}}. Longtime head coach [[Bill Belichick]] led the team for more regular season games (387), post-season games (41) and complete seasons (24) than any other head coach. His 266 wins with the Patriots are far and away the most in franchise history, more than five times those of runner-up [[Mike Holovak]]. Belichick also led the team to nine of their eleven [[Super Bowl]] appearances, winning six of them. Holovak, [[Raymond Berry]] and [[Bill Parcells]] all led the Patriots to league championship games, with only one (Holovak) failing to reach the Super Bowl. Five Patriots head coaches, Holovak, [[Chuck Fairbanks]], Berry, Parcells, and Belichick have been named [[National Football League Coach of the Year Award|coach of the year]] by at least one major news organization. Additionally, Raymond Berry and Bill Parcells are members of the [[Pro Football Hall of Fame]], with Berry being inducted in 1973, and Parcells in 2013.{{cite web | title = Raymond Berry profile | publisher = Pro Football Hall of Fame | url = http://www.profootballhof.com/hof/member.jsp?PLAYER_ID=25 | access-date = April 24, 2008 | archive-date = May 6, 2008 | archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20080506052302/http://www.profootballhof.com/hof/member.jsp?player_id=25 | url-status = live }}{{Cite web |title=Bill Parcells {{!}} Pro Football Hall of Fame |url=https://www.profootballhof.com/players/bill-parcells |access-date=January 12, 2024 |website=pfhof |language=en |archive-date=January 11, 2024 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240111225229/https://www.profootballhof.com/players/bill-parcells/ |url-status=live }}[[File:Bill Belichick 2019 (cropped).jpg|thumb|[[Bill Belichick]] was the most successful head coach in franchise history, leading the team to six [[Super Bowl]] championships in his 24-year tenure.|178x178px]]Twice in Patriots history there were "interim" head coaches. In 1972, John Mazur resigned with five games left in the season.{{cite news |url=http://archive.boston.com/sports/football/patriots/articles/2005/12/01/for_mazur_the_scars_remain/ |title=For Mazur, the scars remain |access-date=May 14, 2008 |last=Grossfeld |first=Stan |date=December 1, 2005 |work=[[The Boston Globe]] |archive-date=October 24, 2012 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20121024115213/http://www.boston.com/sports/football/patriots/articles/2005/12/01/for_mazur_the_scars_remain/?page=1 |url-status=live }} [[Phil Bengtson]] was named as the interim head coach for the rest of the season, during which he only won one game, and he was not made the permanent coach the next year. In 1978, head coach Fairbanks secretly made a deal to leave the team to coach the [[Colorado Buffaloes football|University of Colorado Buffaloes]] while he was still coaching the Patriots. Team owner [[Billy Sullivan (American football)|Billy Sullivan]] suspended Fairbanks for the final game of the regular season, stating "You cannot serve two masters," and [[Ron Erhardt]] and [[Hank Bullough]] took co-head coaching responsibilities for that game.{{cite news |url=http://www.boston.com/sports/football/patriots/superbowl/globe_stories/020502/butt_of_jokes_to_last_laugh+.shtml |title=Butt of jokes to last laugh |access-date=April 26, 2008 |last=Borges |first=Ron |work=[[The Boston Globe]] |archive-date=July 24, 2004 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20040724051437/http://www.boston.com/sports/football/patriots/superbowl/globe_stories/020502/butt_of_jokes_to_last_laugh+.shtml |url-status=dead }} Fairbanks was reinstated when the team qualified for the playoffs, and he lost the first playoff game, his last for the Patriots. Miami Dolphins:
{{Short description|None}} {{Use mdy dates|date=June 2024}} [[File:Don-Shula USO-Tour-USS-Reagan-Address July-13-2009.jpg|thumb|upright|Don Shula on USO tour. He holds NFL record for wins.]] [[File:Mike McDaniel 2023.png|thumb|upright|[[Mike McDaniel]], hired in 2022, was the team's head coach from 2022 to 2025.]] The [[Miami Dolphins]] are a professional [[American football]] franchise based in [[Miami Gardens, Florida|Miami Gardens]], [[Florida]]. They are members of the [[AFC East|East Division]] of the [[American Football Conference]] (AFC) in the [[National Football League]] (NFL). The Dolphins began play in 1966 as an expansion team in the [[American Football League]] (AFL), and joined the NFL as part of the [[AFL–NFL merger]]. The team has played their home games at [[Hard Rock Stadium]], originally known as Joe Robbie Stadium, Pro Player Stadium, Dolphins Stadium, Dolphin Stadium, Landshark Stadium, and Sun Life Stadium, since 1987.{{cite web|url=http://www.dolphinstadium.com/content/history.aspx|title=History|publisher=Dolphin Stadium|access-date=December 6, 2008|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20061024112808/http://www.dolphinstadium.com/content/history.aspx|archive-date=October 24, 2006|url-status=dead}} The Dolphins are currently owned by [[Stephen M. Ross]].{{cite web|url=http://www.miamidolphins.com/newsite/contacts/administration/administration.asp|title=Contacts - Administration|publisher=NFL Enterprises LLC|work=[[Miami Dolphins]]|access-date=December 6, 2008|archive-date=January 21, 2009|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090121133638/http://miamidolphins.com/newsite/contacts/administration/administration.asp|url-status=dead}} There have been fifteen [[head coach]]es for the Dolphins franchise. The team's first head coach was [[George Wilson (football coach)|George Wilson]], who coached for four complete seasons. [[Don Shula]], who coached the Dolphins for 26 consecutive seasons, is the franchise's all-time leader for the most regular-season games coached (392), the most regular-season game wins (257), the most playoff games coached (31), and the most playoff-game wins (17). Shula is also the only Dolphins head coach to win a [[Super Bowl]] with the team, winning two. He was named [[List of NFL Coach of the Year awards|NFL Coach of the Year]] by the [[Associated Press]] (AP), ''[[The Sporting News]]'' (TSN), ''[[Pro Football Weekly]]'' (PFW), and [[United Press International]] (UPI) during his tenure with the Dolphins. The current head coach of the Dolphins, [[Jeff Hafley]], was hired on January 19, 2026.{{Cite web|url=https://www.miamidolphins.com/news/miami-dolphins-agree-to-terms-with-jeff-hafley-as-head-coach|title=Miami Dolphins agree to terms with Jeff Hafley as head coach|publisher=[[Miami Dolphins]]|date=January 19, 2026|accessdate=January 19, 2026}} ==Key== {| class="wikitable" |- |'''#''' |Number of coachesA running total of the number of coaches of the Dolphins. Buffalo Bills:
{{Short description|None}} {{Use mdy dates|date=June 2024}} [[Image:RalphWilsonStadiumsign.jpg|thumb|200px|right|The entrance sign to the home of the Bills, [[Ralph Wilson Stadium|Highmark Stadium]] (formerly New Era Field and Ralph Wilson Stadium)]] The [[Buffalo Bills]] are a professional [[American football]] team based in the [[Buffalo, New York]] [[metropolitan area]]. They are members of the [[AFC East|Eastern Division]] of the [[American Football Conference]] (AFC) in the [[National Football League]] (NFL). The Bills franchise was formed in 1960 as a charter member of the [[American Football League]] (AFL), before joining the NFL as part of the [[AFL-NFL merger]] of 1970.{{cite web|url=http://www.nfl.com/history/chronology/1961-1970|title=NFL History by Decade: 1961–1970|work=[[NFL.com]]|access-date=June 21, 2008|archive-date=February 5, 2007|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20070205052436/http://www.nfl.com/history/chronology/1961-1970|url-status=live}} There have been 21 head coaches for the Bills franchise. [[Buster Ramsey]] became the first head coach of the Buffalo Bills in 1960, serving for two seasons before being fired by Bills owner [[Ralph Wilson]] after the 1961 season.{{cite web|title=The Month of January In Bills History |publisher=Buffalo Bills |url=http://www.buffalobills.com/history/January.jsp |access-date=June 20, 2008 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20080113185105/http://www.buffalobills.com/history/January.jsp |archive-date=January 13, 2008 |url-status=dead }} In terms of tenure, [[Marv Levy]] has coached more games (182) and seasons (12) than any other coach in franchise history. He coached the Bills to four straight [[AFC Championship Game|AFC Championships]] from 1990 to 1993, but failed to lead the team to a victory in the [[Super Bowl]]. One of Levy's predecessors, [[Lou Saban]], who coached the team on two occasions, led the team to the victories in the [[American Football League playoffs|AFL championship in 1964 and 1965]]. Three Bills coaches—Saban, Levy and [[Chuck Knox]]—have been named [[National Football League Coach of the Year Award|coach of the year]] by at least one major news organization.{{cite web|url=http://www.hickoksports.com/history/nflcoy.shtml |title=NFL Coach of the Year Award |last=Hickok |first=Ralph |date=April 17, 2008 |access-date=May 29, 2008 |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://archive.today/20121205094433/http://www.hickoksports.com/history/nflcoy.shtml |archive-date=December 5, 2012 }} Levy and [[Jim Ringo]] are the only Bills coaches to have been inducted into the [[Pro Football Hall of Fame]]. There have been six "interim" head coaches in Bills history. First, in 1968, head coach [[Joe Collier]] was fired two games into the season and replaced by Bills personnel director [[Harvey Johnson (coach)|Harvey Johnson]].{{cite web|title=The Month of September In Bills History |publisher=Buffalo Bills |url=http://www.buffalobills.com/history/September.jsp |access-date=June 20, 2008 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20080607114318/http://www.buffalobills.com/history/September.jsp |archive-date=June 7, 2008 |url-status=dead }} Johnson did not serve as head coach the following season. Then, five games into the 1976 season, Saban unexpectedly resigned as head coach. He was replaced by Ringo, the team's offensive line coach.{{cite web|title=The Month of October In Bills History |publisher=Buffalo Bills |url=http://www.buffalobills.com/history/October.jsp |access-date=June 20, 2008 |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20071219175559/http://www.buffalobills.com/history/October.jsp |archive-date=December 19, 2007 }} Ringo returned to coach the team again in the 1977 season. In October 1985, [[Kay Stephenson]] was fired and replaced by assistant head coach [[Hank Bullough]]. Just over a year later, Bullough was himself fired and replaced by Marv Levy, who had previously served as coach of the Kansas City Chiefs.{{cite web|title=The Month of November In Bills History |publisher=Buffalo Bills |url=http://www.buffalobills.com/history/November.jsp |access-date=June 20, 2008 |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20071218101915/http://www.buffalobills.com/history/November.jsp |archive-date=December 18, 2007 }} Levy would then serve as Bills head coach for the next 12 seasons. In 2016, [[Anthony Lynn]] replaced Rex Ryan for the final game of the season. Following Levy's retirement, the Bills experienced limited success under a series of successive head coaches. [[Wade Phillips]], the Bills' defensive coordinator for the last three years under Levy, took over head coaching duties for the 1998 season. Phillips served as head coach for three seasons, making the playoffs in his first two (He was the last coach to lead the Bills to the playoffs until [[Sean McDermott]] became coach in 2017).{{cite web | title=Dallas Cowboys Head Coach | work=NFL.com | url=http://www.nfl.com/teams/coaches?coaType=head&team=DAL | access-date=November 18, 2009 | archive-date=December 1, 2009 | archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20091201012852/http://www.nfl.com/teams/coaches?coaType=head&team=DAL | url-status=live }} After Phillips' departure following the 2000 season, [[Gregg Williams]] was named head coach.{{cite web | title=Buffalo Bills History | publisher=CBS Interactive | url=http://www.cbssports.com/nfl/teams/history/BUF | access-date=November 18, 2009 | archive-date=August 18, 2011 | archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110818190413/http://www.cbssports.com/nfl/teams/history/BUF | url-status=live }} Williams served as coach for three seasons. At the end of the 2003 season, Williams' contract was not renewed.{{cite news | title=Fewell picked to replace Jauron as Bills coach | publisher=Associated Press | url=https://www.google.com/hostednews/ap/article/ALeqM5j0dS7CbAH7Y4uxHfie2DYOT-9G5QD9C1JN2O0 | access-date=November 18, 2009}}{{dead link|date=June 2024|bot=medic}}{{cbignore|bot=medic}} [[Mike Mularkey]] was named as the new head coach for the 2004 season, leading the Bills to their first winning season since 1999. The Bills experienced less success under Mularkey during 2005, and Mularkey resigned as head coach at the completion of the 2005 season. The Bills then named [[Dick Jauron]] as their head coach for the 2006 season. Jauron was the first coach since Phillips' dismissal with prior head coaching experience, having previously served as head coach of the [[Chicago Bears]] and interim head coach of the [[Detroit Lions]]. Jauron coached the Bills to three consecutive 7–9 seasons before being fired on November 17, 2009, nine games into the 2009 season. Defensive Coordinator Perry Fewell was named as the interim head coach, going 3–4 to finish out the season before all of the Bills coaching staff was fired on January 4, 2010. On January 19, 2010, the Bills named [[Chan Gailey]] as their next head coach; Gailey was fired on December 31, 2012.{{cite news|url=http://www.buffalobills.com/news/article-2/Chan-Gailey-relieved-of-duties-as-head-coach/ab77a208-46ae-4448-adc8-eb8b6500a45b|archive-url=https://archive.today/20130118170438/http://www.buffalobills.com/news/article-2/Chan-Gailey-relieved-of-duties-as-head-coach/ab77a208-46ae-4448-adc8-eb8b6500a45b|url-status=dead|archive-date=January 18, 2013|title=Chan Gailey relieved of duties as head coach|work=[[Buffalo Bills]]|publisher=NFL Enterprises LLC|date=December 31, 2012|access-date=December 31, 2012}} In January 2013, [[Doug Marrone]] was appointed.{{cite news|title=Bills hire Syracuse's Doug Marrone|url=https://www.usatoday.com/story/sports/nfl/bills/2013/01/06/buffalo-bills-hire-syracuse-coach-doug-marrone/1811827/|publisher=USA Today|access-date=January 6, 15, 2015|publisher=Buffalo Bills|access-date=December 27, 2016|date=January 12, 2015}} Rex Ryan was fired from the team on December 27, 2016.{{cite web | title=Bills Announce Coaching Changes | publisher=Buffalo Bills | url=http://www.buffalobills.com/news/article-1/Bills-announce-coaching-changes/99727b25-0911-42e4-a933-d633ccf4e7a0 | access-date=December 27, 2016 }}{{Dead link|date=August 2018 |bot=InternetArchiveBot |fix-attempted=yes }} [[Anthony Lynn]] served as interim head coach until January 11, when the team hired [[Sean McDermott]] to serve in the role on a permanent basis.{{cite web | title=Buffalo Bills agree to hire Sean McDermott | publisher=ESPN | url=http://www.espn.com/nfl/story/_/id/18453441/buffalo-bills-agree-hire-sean-mcdermott-head-coach | access-date=January 12, 2016 | archive-date=January 12, 2017 | archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170112095728/http://www.espn.com/nfl/story/_/id/18453441/buffalo-bills-agree-hire-sean-mcdermott-head-coach | url-status=live }} McDermott's tenure as head coach saw the team qualify for the playoffs eight times. Despite the success, McDermott was fired on January 19, 2026, after nine seasons with the team.{{Cite web |title=Buffalo Bills announce Sean McDermott has been relieved of his duties as head coach |url=https://www.buffalobills.com/news/buffalo-bills-announce-sean-mcdermott-has-been-relieved-of-his-duties-as-head-coach |access-date=January 19, 2026 |website=www.buffalobills.com |language=en-US}} The Bills then named [[Joe Brady (American football coach)|Joe Brady]], the offensive coordinator for the past three seasons under McDermott, as their next head coach, on January 27, 2026.{{cite web |last1=Getzenberg |first1=Alaina |title=Bills promote OC Joe Brady to head coach on 5-year deal |url=https://www.espn.com/nfl/story/_/id/47744752/sources-bills-promoting-oc-brady-head-coach-5-year-deal |website=ESPN.com |publisher=ESPN |access-date=January 27, 2026 |date=January 27, 2026}} ==Key== {| class="wikitable" |- |'''#''' |Number of coaches {{ref label|Note1|A|A}} |- |'''GC''' |Games coached |- |'''W''' |Wins |- |'''L''' |Losses |- |'''T''' |Ties |- |'''Win%''' |[[Winning percentage]] {{ref label|Note1|B|B}} |- |style="background-color:#FFE6BD"| {{0}}{{0}}† |Elected into the [[Pro Football Hall of Fame]] as a coach |- |style="background-color:#E4D00A"|{{0}}{{0}}‡ |Elected into the [[Pro Football Hall of Fame]] as a player |- |style="background-color:#ddffdd"| {{0}}{{0}}* |Spent entire AFL/NFL head coaching career with the Bills |- |style="background-color:#FFFACD"| {{0}}{{0}}** |Two coaching tenures for Lou Saban |- |style="background-color:#FBCEB1"| {{0}}{{0}}^ |Two coaching tenures for Harvey Johnson |} ==Coaches== {| class="wikitable sortable" style="text-align:center" ! Houston Texans:
{{Short description|ALL Houston Texans Head Coaches}} {{Use mdy dates|date=June 2024}} [[Image:Gary Kubiak.jpg|thumb|250px|Gary Kubiak led the Texans to their 1st division championship and 1st playoff win.]] There have been six [[head coaches]] of the [[Houston Texans]], a professional [[American football]] team based in [[Houston, Texas|Houston]], [[Texas]], United States. The Texans play in the [[AFC South|South Division]] of the [[American Football Conference]] (AFC) in the [[National Football League]] (NFL). In [[2002 Houston Texans season|2002]], the Texans entered the NFL as an [[expansion team]], with [[Dom Capers]] (2002–05) as the team's first head coach. Capers was selected, in part, due to his success in having led the expansion [[Carolina Panthers]] to the playoffs in just their [[1996 Carolina Panthers season|second season]] in the league. After three straight seasons of consistent improvement, the Texans took a major step backward in [[2005 Houston Texans season|2005]] with a 2–14 finish. At the end of the season, Capers was fired and replaced with [[Gary Kubiak]] (2006–2013), then the [[offensive coordinator]] for the [[Denver Broncos]]. Kubiak, a Houston native and alumnus of nearby [[Texas A&M University|Texas A&M]], would eventually become the longest-tenured head coach in franchise history, coaching 125 games and posting a 61–64 record. Kubiak earned several distinctions as head coach, having led the Texans to their first non-losing season ([[2007 Houston Texans season|2007]]), their first winning season ([[2009 Houston Texans season|2009]]), and their first division title, playoff appearance, and playoff win ([[2011 Houston Texans season|2011]]). In both 2011 and [[2012 Houston Texans season|2012]], the Texans won the AFC South and the first game of the playoffs but lost the divisional game the following week. In [[2013 Houston Texans season|2013]], the Texans started the season 2–0, but then lost their next 11 games. Kubiak was fired mid-season, on December 6, 2013{{cite web|accessdate=December 6, 2013|url=http://msn.foxsports.com/nfl/story/houston-texans-fire-coach-gary-kubiak-120613|title=Houston Texans fire coach Gary Kubiak|work=Fox Sports }} and was replaced on an interim basis by [[defensive coordinator]] [[Wade Phillips]] (2013, 3 games). During the 2013 off-season, [[Bill O'Brien (American football)|Bill O'Brien]] (2014–2020) was hired to be the Texans' next head coach.{{Cite web|url=https://www.usatoday.com/story/sports/nfl/texans/2013/12/31/houston-texans-agree-bill-obrien-head-coach-penn-state/4273287/|title=Texans reach agreement with Bill O'Brien|website=[[USA Today]]}} O'Brien had previously served as head coach at [[Penn State Football|Penn State]] and as offensive coordinator under [[Bill Belichick]] with the [[New England Patriots]]. In [[2020 Houston Texans season|the 2020 season]], the Texans started 0-4, and O'Brien was fired and replaced by interim head coach [[Romeo Crennel]] during the regular season on October 5, 2020.{{cite web|accessdate=October 5, 2020|url=http://www.houstontexans.com/news/romeo-crennel-brings-wealth-of-experience-as-head-coach|title=Romeo Crennel brings wealth of experience as head coach}} During the 2020 offseason, [[David Culley]] was hired as the Texans' next head coach.{{Cite web|url=https://www.houstontexans.com/news/houston-texans-hire-david-culley-as-head-coach|title=Houston Texans Hire David Culley as Head Coach|website=www.houstontexans.com|language=en-US|access-date=January 29, 2021|date=January 29, 2021|first=Houston Texans|last=Public Relations}} Culley had previously served as an assistant head coach and wide receivers coach with multiple NFL franchises, most recently the [[Baltimore Ravens]]. However, he was fired after just one season, with the team posting a 4–13 record during his brief tenure.{{Cite web|publisher=[[Houston Texans]]|url=https://www.houstontexans.com/news/press-release-statements-from-the-houston-texans|title=Statements from the Houston Texans |date=January 13, 2022|access-date=January 13, 2022}}{{cite web|url=https://www.nfl.com/news/houston-texans-david-culley-fire-head-coach-after-one-season|title=Houston Texans fire head coach David Culley after one season|website=[[NFL.com]]|date=January 13, 2022|access-date=January 13, 2022}} During the 2021 offseason, [[Lovie Smith]] was hired as the Texans' next head coach.{{Cite web|url=https://www.houstontexans.com/news/houston-texans-hire-lovie-smith-as-head-coach|title= Houston Texans Hire Lovie Smith as Head Coach|website=www.houstontexans.com|language=en-US|access-date=February 7, 2022|date=February 7, 2022|first=Houston Texans|last=Public Relations}} Smith had previously served as associate head coach and defensive coordinator during the [[2021 Houston Texans season|2021 season]]. Smith was previously the head coach of the [[Chicago Bears]] and the [[Tampa Bay Buccaneers]], as well as the [[Illinois Fighting Illini football|Illinois Fighting Illini]]. Smith, however, was fired after posting a 3–13–1 record, becoming the second-straight Texans head coach to be fired after one season.{{cite web |url=https://www.houstontexans.com/news/statement-from-texans-chair-and-ceo-cal-mcnair-and-general-manager-nick-caserio- |title=Statement from Texans Chair and CEO Cal McNair and General Manager Nick Caserio on Lovie Smith |publisher=Houston Texans |date=January 8, 2023 |access-date=January 9, 2023}} During the 2022 offseason, [[DeMeco Ryans]] was hired as the Texans' next head coach;{{Cite web|url=https://www.houstontexans.com/news/houston-texans-hire-demeco-ryans-as-head-coach|title=Houston Texans hire DeMeco Ryans as Head Coach|website=www.houstontexans.com|language=en-US|access-date=January 31, 2023|date=January 31, 2023|first=Houston Texans|last=Public Relations}} Ryans served as defensive coordinator with the [[San Francisco 49ers]]. Ryans also was drafted in [[2006 Houston Texans season|2006]] by the Texans where he played for six seasons. ==Key== {| class="wikitable" |- |'''#''' |Number of coachesA running total of the number of coaches of the Texans. New York Jets:
{{Short description|None}} {{Use mdy dates|date=May 2021}} There have been 22 head coaches in the history of the [[New York Jets]] football franchise. The team began as the New York Titans in the [[American Football League]] in 1960, but was renamed the New York Jets three years later.{{cite web |url=http://www.profootballhof.com/history/team.jsp?FRANCHISE_ID=22 |title=New York Jets |access-date=2008-05-25 |publisher=Pro Football Hall of Fame |archive-date=June 2, 2008 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20080602123250/http://www.profootballhof.com/history/team.jsp?FRANCHISE_ID=22 |url-status=live }} The Jets remained in the American Football League until the [[AFL-NFL merger|merger]] with the [[National Football League]] prior to the [[1970 NFL season|1970 season]]. The current head coach of the Jets is [[Aaron Glenn]], who was hired on January 22, 2025.{{Cite web |title=Jets hiring former player, Lions defensive coordinator Aaron Glenn as new head coach |url=https://www.nfl.com/news/jets-hire-aaron-glenn-new-head-coach-lions-defensive-coordinator |access-date=2025-01-22 |website=NFL.com |language=en-US}} [[Sammy Baugh]] became the first head coach of the New York Titans in 1960, serving for two seasons before team owner [[Harry Wismer]] replaced him with [[Bulldog Turner|Clyde "Bulldog" Turner]].{{cite web |url=http://www.newyorkjets.com/team/history/1962.html |title=Year In Review: 1962 |access-date=2008-05-25 |publisher=New York Jets |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20100804014816/http://www.newyorkjets.com/team/history/1962.html |archive-date=2010-08-04 }} In terms of tenure, [[Weeb Ewbank]] has coached more games (158) and more complete seasons (11) than any other head coach in franchise history. He led the Jets to the [[AFL playoffs#1968 Championship|AFL championship in 1968]] and the AFL–NFL championship in [[Super Bowl III]]. [[Walt Michaels]] led the team to the AFC championship game in 1982; he was also honored as the [[Pro Football Weekly NFL Coach of the Year]] and [[UPI NFL Coach of the Year|UPI AFC Coach of the Year]] in 1978. Coaches Baugh, Turner and Ewbank are all members of the Pro Football Hall of Fame; Baugh and Turner were inducted as players, while Ewbank was inducted as a coach/administrator. Three times in Jets history, the team has had an "interim" head coach. In 1975, [[Charley Winner]] was fired as head coach after leading the Jets to a 2–7 record.{{cite web |url=http://www.newyorkjets.com/team/history/1975.html |title=Year In Review: 1975 |access-date=2008-05-25 |publisher=New York Jets |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20100803220900/http://www.newyorkjets.com/team/history/1975.html |archive-date=2010-08-03 }} The team offensive coordinator [[Ken Shipp]] was named the interim coach for the remainder of the season, during which he won only one of five games. Shipp was succeeded by Lou Holtz for the 1976 season.{{cite web |url=http://www.newyorkjets.com/team/history/1976.html |title=Year In Review: 1976 |access-date=2008-05-25 |publisher=New York Jets |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20100804014837/http://www.newyorkjets.com/team/history/1976.html |archive-date=2010-08-04 }} Holtz resigned as Jets head coach with one game left in the 1976 season; [[Mike Holovak]] was named interim coach for the season finale against the [[Cincinnati Bengals]]. In 2024, [[Robert Saleh]] was fired five games into the season, upon which defensive coordinator [[Jeff Ulbrich]] took over as interim head coach.{{cite web |last1=Cimini |first1=Rich |date=October 8, 2024|url=https://www.espn.com/nfl/story/_/id/41679781/jets-fire-coach-robert-saleh-2-3-start-sources-say |title=Jets fire Robert Saleh after 2-3 start, tab Jeff Ulbrich interim|access-date=2024-12-13 |publisher=ESPN }} [[Bill Belichick]] was twice named head coach of the Jets but never coached a single game or practice in that capacity. In 1997, he was named head coach for six days before the deal to allow [[Bill Parcells]] to leave the [[New England Patriots]] for the Jets was brokered, and Belichick became defensive coordinator;{{cite web |title=Parcells to Coach Jets in 1997 |url=https://www.latimes.com/archives/la-xpm-1997-02-11-sp-27483-story.html |website=Los Angeles Times |access-date=May 14, 2021 |date=February 10, 1997 |archive-date=January 26, 2021 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210126094718/https://www.latimes.com/archives/la-xpm-1997-02-11-sp-27483-story.html |url-status=live }} then, when Parcells stepped down after the 1999 season Belichick was named to replace him, but resigned the next day.{{cite news |last1=Shapiro |first1=Leonard |title=Belichick Suddenly Quits Jets After 1 Day |url=https://www.washingtonpost.com/wp-srv/WPcap/2000-01/05/026r-010500-idx.html |newspaper=The Washington Post |access-date=May 14, 2021 |date=January 5, 2000 |archive-date=February 18, 2020 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200218120040/https://www.washingtonpost.com/wp-srv/WPcap/2000-01/05/026r-010500-idx.html |url-status=live }} [[Herman Edwards]] is the only Jets head coach to lead the team to the playoffs more than twice; [[Rex Ryan]] is the only one with more than two postseason wins. [[Todd Bowles]] and [[Robert Saleh]] are the only ones to coach the Jets for more than two seasons without making the playoffs. Two Jets head coaches have played for the Jets; [[Walt Michaels]], who as a defensive assistant coach had to suit up and play in the 1963 season opener due to injuries, and [[Aaron Glenn]], a 1994 first-round draft pick who played cornerback for the team from 1994 to 2001. ==Key== {| class="wikitable" |- |'''#''' |Number of coachesA running total of the number of Jets head coaches. Baltimore Ravens:
{{Short description|None}} {{Use mdy dates|date=June 2024}} [[Image:Mandtbankstade.png|thumb|300px|Satellite picture of the Ravens' home, [[M&T Bank Stadium]]]] [[File:John Harbaugh 2012.jpg|upright|thumb|[[John Harbaugh]] is the longest-serving head coach of the Ravens, coaching the franchise from [[2008 NFL season|2008]] to [[2025 NFL season|2025]]. He is the most successful head coach in franchise history.]] The [[Baltimore Ravens]] are an [[American football]] team based in [[Baltimore|Baltimore, Maryland]]. They are currently members of the North division of the [[American Football Conference]] (AFC) in the [[National Football League]] (NFL). In 1996, the Ravens joined the [[American Football Conference]] (AFC) of the [[National Football League]] (NFL) after former [[Cleveland Browns]] owner [[Art Modell]] decided to relocate the team to Baltimore. However, as part of an agreement between Modell, the NFL and the city of Cleveland, Modell left the Browns' name, colors and history in Cleveland. However, the team's players and staff were permitted to move to Baltimore with the franchise. For this reason, the Ravens are considered an [[expansion team]]. The Browns name was later reintroduced in 1999.{{cite news|url=https://query.nytimes.com/gst/fullpage.html?res=9A07E1DC1539F933A25751C0A960958260|title=N.F.L. Gives Modell a Ticket to Baltimore|last=Sandomir|first=Richard|date=February 10, 1996|work=[[The New York Times]]|access-date=May 25, 2008}} Modell had planned to bring [[Bill Belichick]] to Baltimore as head coach, but Belichick was fired February 14, 1996. Instead, Modell hired [[Ted Marchibroda]] on February 15 as the first head coach of the Ravens franchise in 1996. Marchibroda had previously coached the [[Baltimore Colts]] in the 1970s and the [[Indianapolis Colts]] in the 1990s. After three seasons, his contract was not renewed. [[Brian Billick]] succeeded him after accepting a six-year coaching contract from Modell. Billick went on to lead the Ravens to four playoff appearances in his nine years as coach. In 2000, he led the Ravens to a 34–7 victory in [[Super Bowl XXXV]] against the [[New York Giants]].{{cite web|url=http://www.baltimoreravens.com/News/History/Baltimore_Football_History.aspx|title=Baltimore Football History|publisher=Baltimore Ravens|access-date=May 25, 2008|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20100211225719/http://www.baltimoreravens.com/News/History/Baltimore_Football_History.aspx|archive-date=February 11, 2010|url-status=dead}} On December 31, 2007, Billick was fired by Ravens owner [[Steve Bisciotti]] after leading the Ravens to a 5–11 record in the [[2007 NFL season|2007 season]]. Less than three weeks later, the Ravens hired [[John Harbaugh]] as the franchise's third head coach.{{cite news|url=http://nbcsports.msnbc.com/id/22734839/|title=Ravens settle for Harbaugh as their new coach|date=January 18, 2008|work=[[Associated Press]]|access-date=May 25, 2008|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20080514120836/http://nbcsports.msnbc.com/id/22734839/|archive-date=May 14, 2008|url-status=dead}} Harbaugh reached the playoffs in each of his first five seasons as Ravens head coach, bringing success to the franchise, including a victory in [[Super Bowl XLVII]], in which the Ravens beat the [[San Francisco 49ers]], led by John's brother [[Jim Harbaugh]], by a score of 34–31. ==Coaches== {| class="wikitable" |- |'''#''' |Number of coachesA running total of the number of coaches of the Ravens. Cincinnati Bengals:
{{Short description|None}} {{Use mdy dates|date=June 2024}} [[File:Cincinnati-paul-brown-stadium.jpg|thumb|The Bengals have played in [[Paul Brown Stadium]] since 2000.]] This is a '''complete list of Cincinnati Bengals head coaches'''. There have been ten head coaches for the [[Cincinnati Bengals]] of the [[National Football League]] (NFL). A professional [[American football]] team based in [[Cincinnati, Ohio]], the Bengals are a member of the [[AFC North|North Division]] of the [[American Football Conference]] (AFC). The Bengals franchise was founded in 1968 as a member of the Western Division of the [[American Football League]] (AFL),{{cite web|title=History – Cincinnati Bengals|url=http://www.bengals.com/team/history.html|work=[[Cincinnati Bengals]]|access-date=June 15, 2011|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20140106223648/http://www.bengals.com/team/history.html|archive-date=January 6, 2014|url-status=dead}} before merging with the NFL in [[AFL-NFL merger|1970]]. The current head coach is [[Zac Taylor]], who was hired after the 2018 season. Taylor replaced [[Marvin Lewis]], who remains the Bengals all-time leader in seasons coached, games coached, wins, and playoff games coached. Three coaches have won a conference championship with the team: [[Forrest Gregg]] in 1981, [[Sam Wyche]] in 1988 and [[Zac Taylor|Taylor]] in 2021.{{cite web|title=Cincinnati Bengals Championship History|url=http://www.nflteamhistory.com/nfl_teams/cincinnati_bengals/championship_history.html|work=NFL Team History.com|access-date=June 15, 2011}} Gregg leads all coaches in winning percentage with .561.{{cite web|title=Forrest Gregg Records, Statistics, and Category Ranks|url=https://www.pro-football-reference.com/coaches/GregFo0.htm|work=[[Pro-Football-Reference.com]]|publisher=Sports Reference|access-date=June 15, 2011}} [[Dick LeBeau]] had the lowest winning percentage, with .267.{{cite web|title=Dick LeBeau Records, Statistics, and Category Ranks|url=https://www.pro-football-reference.com/coaches/LeBeDi0.htm|work=[[Pro-Football-Reference.com]]|publisher=Sports Reference|access-date=June 15, 2011}} Of the ten Bengals head coaches, three have been elected to the [[Pro Football Hall of Fame]]: [[Paul Brown]], [[Forrest Gregg]], and [[Dick LeBeau]] (although only Brown was inducted as a coach, the other two were inducted as players).{{cite web|title=Hall of Famers by Franchise|url=http://www.profootballhof.com/hof/teams.aspx|publisher=[[Pro Football Hall of Fame]]|access-date=June 15, 2011}} Two former players have been head coach for the Bengals: [[Sam Wyche]] and [[Bruce Coslet]]. ==Key== {| class="wikitable" |- |'''#''' |Number of coachesA running total of the number of coaches of the Bengals. Cleveland Browns:
{{Short description|None}} {{Use mdy dates|date=June 2024}} [[File:Cleveland National Air Show (43805784984).jpg|thumb|300x300px|The Browns have played their home games at Cleveland Browns Stadium since 1999.]] The [[Cleveland Browns]] are a professional [[American football]] franchise based in [[Cleveland, Ohio|Cleveland]], [[Ohio]]. They are a member of the [[AFC North|North Division]] of the [[American Football Conference]] (AFC) in the [[National Football League]] (NFL). The team began playing in 1946 as a charter member of the [[All-America Football Conference]] (AAFC), and joined the NFL as part of the [[1950 NFL season#The AAFC–NFL merger|AAFC–NFL merger]] in 1950.{{cite web |url=http://www.clevelandbrowns.com/team/history/results.php |title=Year By Year Season Results |publisher=NFL Enterprises LLC |work=[[Cleveland Browns]] |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20100402101044/http://www.clevelandbrowns.com/team/history/results.php |archive-date=April 2, 2010 |url-status=dead |access-date=January 22, 2013}} The team played their home games at [[Cleveland Stadium]] from 1946 to 1995 before moving to [[Cleveland Browns Stadium]], where they have played since 1999.{{Cite web |url=http://www.clevelandbrowns.com/tickets/stadium/ |title=Cleveland Browns Stadium |publisher=NFL Enterprises LLC |work=Cleveland Browns |access-date=January 22, 2013 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20100315201301/http://www.clevelandbrowns.com/tickets/stadium/ |archive-date=March 15, 2010 |url-status=dead }} The Browns did not play from 1996 to 1998 when the team's owner, [[Art Modell]], moved the team to [[Baltimore, Maryland|Baltimore]], [[Maryland]] and formed the [[Baltimore Ravens]]. The team was reactivated under new ownership in Cleveland in 1999.{{harvnb|Henkel|2005|p=111}} The Browns are currently owned by [[Jimmy Haslam III]].{{cite web |url=http://www.clevelandbrowns.com/team/front-office.html |date=January 20, 2013 |title=Cleveland Browns – Front Office |access-date=January 22, 2013 |archive-date=January 23, 2013 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130123205511/http://www.clevelandbrowns.com/team/front-office.html |url-status=live }} There have been 19 non-interim [[head coach]]es for the Browns franchise. Their first head coach was [[Paul Brown]], who coached for 17 complete seasons. Brown is also the franchise's all-time leader for the most regular season games coached (214), the most regular season game wins (158), the most playoffs games coached (14), and the most playoff game wins (9). Brown is the only Browns head coach to win an AAFC championship, winning four. Brown and [[Blanton Collier]] have led the Browns to NFL championships. Brown won the ''[[Sporting News]]'' [[National Football League Coach of the Year Award|NFL Coach of the Year]] three times, the [[United Press International]] (UPI) NFL Coach of the Year once,{{cite web|url=http://www.hickoksports.com/history/nflcoy.shtml |title=NFL Coach of the Year Award |last=Hickok |first=Ralph |date=April 17, 2008 |publisher=Hick OK Sports |url-status=dead |access-date=January 22, 2013 |archive-url=https://archive.today/20121205094433/http://www.hickoksports.com/history/nflcoy.shtml |archive-date=December 5, 2012 }} and was elected into the [[Pro Football Hall of Fame]] as a coach. Five coaches have won Coach of the Year awards: Brown ([[1949 NFL season|1949]], [[1951 NFL season|1951]], [[1953 NFL season|1953]], [[1957 NFL season|1957]]), [[Forrest Gregg]] ([[1976 NFL season|1976]]), [[Sam Rutigliano]] ([[1979 NFL season|1979]], [[1980 NFL season|1980]]), [[Marty Schottenheimer]] ([[1986 NFL season|1986]]), and [[Kevin Stefanski]] ([[2020 NFL season|2020]], [[2023 NFL season|2023]]). Since their reactivation in 1999, the Browns have had significant turnover at the head coaching position. Eleven men have coached the Browns since 1999, and only two have reached the postseason: [[Butch Davis]] ([[2002 Cleveland Browns season|2002]]) and Stefanski ([[2020 Cleveland Browns season|2020]] and [[2023 Cleveland Browns season|2023]]). ==Key== {| class="wikitable" |- |'''#''' |Number of coachesA running total of the number of coaches of the Browns. Thus, any coach who has two or more terms as head coach is only counted once. Indianapolis Colts:
{{Short description|None}} {{Use mdy dates|date=June 2024}} [[File:Tony Dungy Navy.jpg|200px|thumb|upright|[[Tony Dungy]] is a former head coach of the Colts. He led them to their second Super Bowl victory in Miami ([[Super Bowl XLI|XLI]]).]] The [[Indianapolis Colts]] are a professional [[American football]] team based in [[Indianapolis]], [[Indiana]]. They are a member of the [[American Football Conference South Division|South Division]] of the [[American Football Conference]] (AFC) in the [[National Football League]] (NFL). In 1953, a [[Baltimore, Maryland|Baltimore]]-based group led by [[Carroll Rosenbloom]] won the rights to a new Baltimore franchise. Rosenbloom was granted an NFL team, and was awarded the holdings of the defunct [[Dallas Texans (NFL)|Dallas Texans]] organization. The team was known as the Baltimore Colts for 31 seasons before moving to [[Indianapolis, Indiana|Indianapolis]] in March 1984.{{cite web |url=http://www.profootballhof.com/history/team.jsp?franchise_id=14 |title=Indianapolis Colts |access-date=June 18, 2008 |publisher=Pro Football Hall of Fame |archive-date=June 12, 2008 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20080612154940/http://www.profootballhof.com/history/team.jsp?franchise_id=14 |url-status=live }} There have been 19{{efn|Although [[Bruce Arians]] was named as interim head coach during the 2012 season, he was not credited with the team's record while in charge.}} head coaches for the Colts franchise. [[Keith Molesworth]] became the first coach of the Baltimore Colts in 1953, but he was reassigned to a different position with the team following the season.{{cite web |url=http://www.sportsecyclopedia.com/nfl/balticolts/baltcolts.html |title=Baltimore Colts: Historical Moments |access-date=June 18, 2008 |publisher=Baltimore Colts |archive-date=February 10, 2007 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20070210173055/http://www.sportsecyclopedia.com/nfl/balticolts/baltcolts.html |url-status=live }} In terms of tenure, [[Weeb Ewbank]] has led the team for more games (112) and more complete seasons (nine) than any other head coach. He led the team to two of their [[List of NFL champions|NFL championships]]. Four Colts head coaches; Ewbank, [[Don Shula]] (3), [[Ted Marchibroda]], and [[Tony Dungy]] have been named [[National Football League Coach of the Year Award|coach of the year]] by at least one major news organization. Ewbank, Shula, and Dungy are members of the [[Pro Football Hall of Fame]], having been inducted in 1978, 1997, and 2016 respectively.{{cite news |url=https://www.cbsnews.com/news/coach-ewbank-laid-to-rest/ |title=Coach Ewbank Laid To Rest |access-date=June 19, 2008 |publisher=CBS News |date=November 18, 1998 |archive-date=May 24, 2011 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110524133811/http://www.cbsnews.com/stories/1998/11/18/archive/main22907.shtml |url-status=live }}{{cite web|url=http://www.nationwidespeakers.com/speaker/89/don-shula/nfl-coaching-legend |title=Don Shula: NFL Coaching Legend |access-date=June 19, 2008 |publisher=Nationwide |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20080517043518/http://www.nationwidespeakers.com/speaker/89/don-shula/nfl-coaching-legend |archive-date=May 17, 2008 }} Six times in Colts history there were interim head coaches. In 1972, [[Don McCafferty]] was fired five games into the season. [[John Sandusky]] was named as the interim head coach for the rest of the season, during which he led the Colts to a 4–5 record, but he was not made the permanent coach the next year. In 1974, head coach [[Howard Schnellenberger]] started off the season 0–3 and was fired. [[Joe Thomas (football coach)|Joe Thomas]] assumed the duties of head coach and finished the season at 2–12. In 1991, the Colts started off 0–5 and [[Ron Meyer]] was fired as head coach. [[Rick Venturi]] was named as the interim for the final 11 games.{{cite web |url=https://www.sportsecyclopedia.com/nfl/indy/icolts.html |title=Indianapolis Colts: Historical Moments |access-date=June 19, 2008 |publisher=Indianapolis Colts |archive-date=September 9, 2023 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230909062525/https://sportsecyclopedia.com/nfl/indy/icolts.html |url-status=live }} In 2005 [[Tony Dungy]] was forced to miss one game due to [[2005 Indianapolis Colts season#Tony Dungy and family experience tragedy|personal issues]]. [[Jim Caldwell (American football)|Jim Caldwell]] was named as the one game interim.{{cite web |url=https://www.espn.com/nfl/news/story?id=2268593 |title=Dungy's son, 18, found dead in Tampa suburb |date=December 22, 2005 |access-date=June 18, 2008 |publisher=ESPN |archive-date=May 22, 2008 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20080522084634/http://sports.espn.go.com/nfl/news/story?id=2268593 |url-status=live }} In 2012 offensive coordinator [[Bruce Arians]] was named as the interim head coach indefinitely after [[Chuck Pagano]] was diagnosed with [[leukemia]] four weeks into the 2012 NFL season.{{cite web |url=https://www.espn.com/nfl/story/_/id/8447831/indianapolis-colts-coach-chuck-pagano-leukemia |title=Chuck Pagano battling leukemia |date=October 2012 |access-date=October 1, 2012 |publisher=ESPN |archive-date=October 3, 2012 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20121003005429/http://espn.go.com/nfl/story/_/id/8447831/indianapolis-colts-coach-chuck-pagano-leukemia |url-status=live }} Arians led the Colts to a 9–3 record – the record being credited to Pagano – and made the playoffs. Following a 3–5–1 start, Frank Reich was fired and on November 7, 2022, the Indianapolis Colts named Jeff Saturday as the team's interim coach for the rest of the season, during which he led the Colts to a 1–7 record. Washington Commanders:
{{Short description|None}} {{Use mdy dates|date=June 2024}} [[File:Dan Quinn training camp 2025.jpg|thumb|right|[[Dan Quinn (American football)|Dan Quinn]], Washington's current head coach, was hired in 2024.]] The [[Washington Commanders]] are a professional [[American football]] franchise based in the [[Washington metropolitan area]]. They are members of the [[NFC East|East]] division in the [[National Football Conference]] (NFC) of the [[National Football League]] (NFL). The Commanders were founded in {{nfly|1932}} as the [[Boston]] Braves, named after the [[History of the Boston Braves|local baseball franchise]].{{cite web|title=Washington Commanders Team History|url=https://www.profootballhof.com/teams/washington-commanders/team-history/|website=[[Pro Football Hall of Fame]]|access-date=March 14, 2024|language=en}} The franchise changed its name the following year to the Redskins and moved to [[Washington, D.C.]] in {{nfly|1937}}. In {{nfly|2020}}, the team retired the Redskins name after [[Washington Redskins name controversy|longstanding controversies]] surrounding it and briefly played as the Washington Football Team before becoming the Commanders in {{nfly|2022}}. There have been 31 head coaches for the franchise, four of which played for the franchise during their career. [[Joe Gibbs]] is the only coach to have more than one tenure with the team.{{Cite web |title=Joe Gibbs' Coaching Record|url=https://www.pro-football-reference.com/coaches/GibbJo0.htm|access-date=March 26, 2024|website=[[Pro-Football-Reference.com]]|language=en}} [[Ray Flaherty]] coached teams that won the [[1937 NFL Championship Game|1937]] and [[1942 NFL Championship]]s, while Joe Gibbs coached teams that won [[Super Bowl XVII]] (1982), [[Super Bowl XXII]] (1987), and [[Super Bowl XXVI]] (1991).{{cite web|work=NFLTeamHistory.com |url=http://www.nflteamhistory.com/nfl_teams/washington_redskins/championship_history.html |title=Washington Redskins Championship History |access-date=March 14, 2008 |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20080422032749/http://www.nflteamhistory.com/nfl_teams/washington_redskins/championship_history.html |archive-date=April 22, 2008 }} Gibbs is the franchise leader in games coached and wins, with [[Dudley DeGroot]] leading in [[winning percentage]] with .737.{{Cite web |title=Dudley DeGroot's Coaching Record|url=https://www.pro-football-reference.com/coaches/DeGrDu0.htm|access-date=March 26, 2024|website=[[Pro-Football-Reference.com]]|language=en}} Seven are [[Pro Football Hall of Fame]] members: Flaherty, [[Turk Edwards]], [[Curly Lambeau]], [[Otto Graham]], [[Vince Lombardi]], [[George Allen (American football coach)|George Allen]], and Gibbs.{{cite web | title=Hall of Famers by Franchise | work=[[Pro Football Hall of Fame]] | url=http://www.profootballhof.com/hof/teams.aspx | access-date=March 14, 2008 }} ==Coaches== {| class="wikitable" |- |'''#''' |Number of coaches{{efn|A running total of the number of coaches of the Redskins. Dallas Cowboys:
{{Short description|None}} {{Use mdy dates|date=June 2024}} [[File:Tom Landry Jan 1997.jpg|200px|thumb|right|Tom Landry was the first head coach of the Cowboys, and coached the team from 1960 to 1988. He was inducted into the Pro Football Hall of Fame in 1990.]] The [[Dallas Cowboys]] are a professional [[American football]] team based in [[Frisco, Texas]]. Their stadium is located in [[Arlington, Texas]]. They are members of the [[NFC East|Eastern Division]] of the [[National Football Conference]] (NFC) in the [[National Football League]] (NFL). The Cowboys franchise was founded in 1960 as an expansion team.{{cite web | work=[[Dallas Cowboys]] | url=http://www.dallascowboys.com/history_year.cfm | title=1950 Dallas Cowboys | access-date=March 15, 2008 |archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20080308062403/http://www.dallascowboys.com/history_year.cfm |archive-date = March 8, 2008}} The team played their games in the [[Cotton Bowl (stadium)|Cotton Bowl]] from 1960 to 1970, then in [[Texas Stadium]] from 1971 to 2008, and [[AT&T Stadium]] from 2009 to present. There have been nine [[head coach]]es for the Dallas Cowboys. Three coaches have won Super Bowls with the team: [[Tom Landry]] in [[Super Bowl VI]] and [[Super Bowl XII|XII]], [[Jimmy Johnson (American football coach)|Jimmy Johnson]] in [[Super Bowl XXVII]] and [[Super Bowl XXVIII|XXVIII]], and [[Barry Switzer]] in [[Super Bowl XXX]].{{cite web | work=NFLTeamHistory.com | url=http://www.nflteamhistory.com/nfl_teams/dallas_cowboys/championship_history.html | title=Dallas Cowboys Championship History | access-date=March 15, 2008}} Landry is the team's all-time leader in games coached and wins, and Switzer leads all coaches in regular season [[winning percentage]] with .625. [[Dave Campo]] is the only Cowboys coach with a losing record (.313), and is also the only coach in franchise history to have never posted a winning season. The team's first coach, Tom Landry, has been inducted into the [[Pro Football Hall of Fame]].{{cite web | title=Hall of Famers by Franchise | work=[[Pro Football Hall of Fame]] | url=http://www.profootballhof.com/hof/teams.aspx | access-date=March 15, 2008 }} The current coach is [[Brian Schottenheimer]], who replaced [[Mike McCarthy]] on January 24, 2025, after the latter's contract expired on January 13. ==Key== {| class="wikitable" |- |'''#''' |Number of coachesA running total of the number of coaches of the Cowboys. Philadelphia Eagles:
{{Short description|None}} {{Use mdy dates|date=June 2024}} This is a '''list of head coaches for the Philadelphia Eagles'''. The [[Philadelphia Eagles]] are a professional [[American football]] team based in [[Philadelphia]], [[Pennsylvania]]. The Eagles joined the [[National Football League]] (NFL) as an [[expansion team]] in 1933. Currently members of the [[NFC East|Eastern Division]] of the [[National Football Conference]] (NFC), the team has won five [[List of NFL champions|NFL titles]] and made five [[Super Bowl]] appearances ([[Super Bowl XV|1980]], [[Super Bowl XXXIX|2004]], [[Super Bowl LII|2017]], [[Super Bowl LVII|2022]], and [[Super Bowl LIX|2024]]), with their first Super Bowl victory coming in [[Super Bowl LII]] under [[Doug Pederson]] and their second coming in [[Super Bowl LIX]] under [[Nick Sirianni]]. Four coaches have won [[History of National Football League Championship|NFL championships]] with the team: [[Greasy Neale|Earl "Greasy" Neale]] in [[1948 NFL Championship Game|1948]] and [[1949 NFL Championship Game|1949]], [[Buck Shaw]] in [[1960 NFL Championship Game|1960]], Doug Pederson in [[Super Bowl LIX|2017]], and Nick Sirianni in [[Super Bowl LIX|2024]].{{cite web |publisher=NFLTeamHistory.com |url=http://www.nflteamhistory.com/nfl_teams/philadelphia_eagles/championship_history.html |title=Philadelphia Eagles Championship History |access-date=March 15, 2008 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20080517082645/http://www.nflteamhistory.com/nfl_teams/philadelphia_eagles/championship_history.html |archive-date=May 17, 2008 |url-status=dead }} [[Andy Reid]] is the all-time leader in games coached and wins,{{cite web|url=https://www.pro-football-reference.com/coaches/ReidAn0.htm|title=Andy Reid's Coaching Record|publisher=Pro Football Reference|access-date=March 16, 2008|archive-date=September 18, 2010|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20100918113035/http://www.pro-football-reference.com/coaches/ReidAn0.htm|url-status=live}} while Sirianni has the highest winning percentage with .676 (with at least one full season coached). [[Bert Bell]] is statistically the worst coach the Eagles have had in terms of winning percentage, with a .185 win/loss percentage.{{cite web|url=https://www.pro-football-reference.com/coaches/BellBe0.htm |title=Bert Bell's Coaching Record |publisher=Pro Football Reference |access-date=March 16, 2008 |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20080303235621/http://www.pro-football-reference.com/coaches/BellBe0.htm |archive-date=March 3, 2008 }} Of the 24 Eagles coaches, five have been elected into the [[Pro Football Hall of Fame]].{{cite web |title=Hall of Famers by Franchise |publisher=[[Pro Football Hall of Fame]] |url=http://www.profootballhof.com/hof/teams.aspx |access-date=March 14, 2008 |archive-date=October 15, 2015 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20151015213446/http://www.profootballhof.com/hof/teams.aspx |url-status=live }} [[Bert Bell]] was a charter member of the Hall of Fame. Bell was inducted for his work as the NFL Commissioner from 1946 to 1959. [[Wayne Millner]], who coached the team in 1951, was enshrined as a player in 1968. [[Greasy Neale]] was in the class of 1969 for his work as the Eagles coach in the 1940s. [[Mike McCormack (American football)|Mike McCormack]] made the 1984 class for his Offensive Tackle play. [[Dick Vermeil]] entered the [[Pro Football Hall of Fame]] in 2022 for his coaching career with the Eagles, Rams and Chiefs. Several former NFL players have been head coaches for the Eagles, including [[Jerry Williams (American football)|Jerry Williams]], [[Ed Khayat]], and [[Marion Campbell]]. Andy Reid{{cite magazine |magazine=[[Sports Illustrated]] |url=http://sportsillustrated.cnn.com/football/nfl/1998/playoffs/news/1999/01/11/pk_mmqb/ |title=The Racial Scorecard |access-date=March 15, 2008 |date=January 11, 1999 |archive-date=December 18, 2007 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20071218083016/http://sportsillustrated.cnn.com/football/nfl/1998/playoffs/news/1999/01/11/pk_mmqb/ |url-status=dead }} spent 14 seasons in charge before he was fired on December 31, 2012, after a 4–12 season – Reid's worst season in charge – which left the Eagles bottom of the NFC.{{cite news|url=http://www.philadelphiaeagles.com/news/article-1/Eagles-Announce-End-Of-Reids-Tenure/02b4c730-d341-4dd5-aa03-fecd9728c4ba|title=Eagles Announce End Of Reid's Tenure|work=[[Philadelphia Eagles]]|publisher=NFL Enterprises LLC|date=December 31, 2012|access-date=December 31, 2012|url-status=dead|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130102114214/http://www.philadelphiaeagles.com/news/article-1/Eagles-Announce-End-Of-Reids-Tenure/02b4c730-d341-4dd5-aa03-fecd9728c4ba|archive-date=January 2, 2013}}{{cite magazine |magazine=[[Sports Illustrated]] |url=http://sportsillustrated.cnn.com/football/nfl/1998/playoffs/news/1999/01/11/pk_mmqb/ |title=The Racial Scorecard |access-date=March 15, 2008 |date=January 11, 1999 |archive-date=December 18, 2007 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20071218083016/http://sportsillustrated.cnn.com/football/nfl/1998/playoffs/news/1999/01/11/pk_mmqb/ |url-status=dead }} He was replaced by former [[Oregon Ducks football|University of Oregon]] head coach [[Chip Kelly]], who led the Eagles to a 10–6 record and the playoffs. Kelly was fired on December 29, 2015, after going 6–9 through that season's first 15 games. He was replaced by offensive coordinator [[Pat Shurmur]] for Week 17. [[Doug Pederson]] served five seasons with the Eagles as their head coach before being fired on January 11, 2021, after leading the team to a 4–11–1 record in his final year as head coach. [[Nick Sirianni]] has been head coach since January 24, 2021.{{cite web | last=Spadaro | first=Dave | title=Eagles announce Nick Sirianni as head coach | website=Philadelphia Eagles | date=2021-01-24 | url=https://www.philadelphiaeagles.com/news/eagles-announce-nick-sirianni-as-head-coach | access-date=2026-04-24}} ==Key== {| class="wikitable" style="font-size:90%;" |- |'''#''' |Number of coaches{{refn|A running total of the number of coaches of the Eagles. New York Giants:
{{Short description|None}} {{Use mdy dates|date=May 2021}} {{Use American English|date=September 2022}} The [[New York Giants]] are a professional [[American football]] team based in [[East Rutherford, New Jersey]]. They are members of the [[NFC East|East Division]] of the [[National Football Conference]] (NFC) in the [[National Football League]] (NFL). The franchise was founded in {{nfly|1925}} and have played for 19 [[History of National Football League Championship|NFL championships]]. They have won seven World Championship Games ([[Super Bowl]] and NFL Championship games) and one NFL Championship by virtue of having the league's best record at the end of the season in 1927.{{cite news |url=https://www.nytimes.com/2005/10/25/sports/football/25cnd-mara.html |title=Wellington Mara, Co-Owner of New York Giants, Is Dead at 89 |access-date=2008-04-04 |last=Goldstein |first=Richard |date=2005-10-25 |work=New York Times |archive-date=March 11, 2012 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120311033645/http://www.nytimes.com/2005/10/25/sports/football/25cnd-mara.html |url-status=live }}{{cite web | work=NFLTeamHistory.com | url=http://www.nflteamhistory.com/nfl_teams/new_york_giants/championship_history.html | title=New York Giants Championship History | access-date=2008-03-16 | archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20080517082901/http://www.nflteamhistory.com/nfl_teams/new_york_giants/championship_history.html | archive-date=2008-05-17 | url-status=dead }} There have been 22 head coaches for the Giants franchise. Five coaches have won [[NFL Championships]] with the team: [[Earl Potteiger]] in {{nfly|1927}}, [[Steve Owen (American football)|Steve Owen]] in [[1934 NFL Championship Game|1934]] and [[1938 NFL Championship Game|1938]], [[Jim Lee Howell]] in [[1956 NFL Championship Game|1956]], [[Bill Parcells]] in [[Super Bowl XXI|1986]] and [[Super Bowl XXV|1990]], and [[Tom Coughlin]] in [[Super Bowl XLII|2007]] and [[Super Bowl XLVI|2011]]. Steve Owen leads all-time in games coached and wins, and [[LeRoy Andrews]] leads all coaches in winning percentage with .828 (with at least one full season coached).{{cite web|url=https://www.pro-football-reference.com/coaches/AndrRo0.htm|title=LeRoy Andrews' Coaching Record|publisher=Pro Football Reference|access-date=2008-03-17|archive-date=February 19, 2008|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20080219180034/http://www.pro-football-reference.com/coaches/AndrRo0.htm|url-status=live}} [[Bill Arnsparger]] is statistically the worst coach the Giants have had in terms of winning percentage, with .200.{{cite web|url=https://www.pro-football-reference.com/coaches/ArnsBi0.htm|title=Bill Arnsparger's Coaching Record|publisher=Pro Football Reference|access-date=2008-03-17|archive-date=December 22, 2017|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20171222051428/https://www.pro-football-reference.com/coaches/ArnsBi0.htm|url-status=live}} Of the 22 Giants coaches, three have been elected into the [[Pro Football Hall of Fame]]: [[Benny Friedman]], Steve Owen and Bill Parcells.{{cite web | title=Hall of Famers by Franchise | work=[[Pro Football Hall of Fame]] | url=http://www.profootballhof.com/hof/teams.aspx | access-date=March 16, 2008 | archive-date=October 15, 2015 | archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20151015213446/http://www.profootballhof.com/hof/teams.aspx | url-status=live }} Several former players have been head coach for the Giants, including [[Doc Alexander]], Earl Potteiger, Benny Friedman, Steve Owen, [[Jim Lee Howell]], and [[Alex Webster (gridiron football)|Alex Webster]]. The current head coach of the Giants is [[John Harbaugh]], since his hiring on January 17, 2026.{{Cite web|url=https://www.giants.com/news/john-harbaugh-hired-head-coach-new-york-giants-nfl|title=New York Giants hire John Harbaugh as head coach|first=Dan|last=Salomone|publisher=[[New York Giants]]|date=January 19, 2026|accessdate=January 19, 2026}} ==Key== {| class="wikitable" |- |'''#''' |Number of coachesA running total of the number of Giants head coaches. Jacksonville Jaguars:
{{Short description|none}} {{Use mdy dates|date=June 2024}} [[File:Army.Coughlin.jpg|thumb|200px|right|Tom Coughlin, the Jaguars' first coach from {{nfly|1995}} to {{nfly|2002}}, is also the most successful coach in Jaguars team history, as measured by playoff appearances, conference championship appearances, and overall winning percentage of games won.]] The [[Jacksonville Jaguars]] are a professional [[American football]] franchise based in [[Jacksonville, Florida]]. They are members of the [[AFC South|South Division]] of the [[American Football Conference]] (AFC) in the [[National Football League]] (NFL). The team, along with the [[Carolina Panthers]], joined the NFL as [[expansion team]]s in 1995. The Jaguars, along with the [[Houston Texans]], have never played in a Super Bowl or any other NFL Championship, but have made 3 appearances in AFC Championship games; twice against the New England Patriots after the 1996 and the 2017 seasons, and once against the Tennessee Titans after the 1999 season. The Jaguars have had nine head coaches since their inaugural [[1995 Jacksonville Jaguars season|1995 season]], including two interim coaches. [[Tom Coughlin]] and [[Jack Del Rio]] each won 68 games while coaching the Jaguars, though Coughlin is more successful in terms of [[winning percentage]], winning 53.1% of his games in charge. Their current head coach is [[Liam Coen]], who was hired on January 24, 2025.{{Cite web|url=https://www.jaguars.com/news/k000151-jacksonville-jaguars-agree-to-terms-with-liam-coen-to-become-head-coach|title=Jacksonville Jaguars Agree to Terms with Liam Coen to Become Head Coach|publisher=[[Jacksonville Jaguars]]|date=January 24, 2025|access-date=January 24, 2025}} ==History== ===Tom Coughlin era (1995–2002)=== The Jaguars first head coach was [[Tom Coughlin]], who was hired by owner [[Wayne Weaver]] well over a year before the team's inaugural season in 1995. The Jaguars won four games their first season, surpassing the previous record of wins for an expansion team (three), but falling well short of the [[Carolina Panthers]] record-setting pace that same year of seven wins. The next year, 1996, Coughlin and the Jaguars went to the AFC Championship game, a feat repeated in 1999, but both years the Jaguars fell short of the Super Bowl. In 2002, Coughlin was fired. ===Jack Del Rio era (2003–2011)=== [[File:Jack del Rio 2008.jpg|thumb|100px|left|Jack Del Rio is the longest-serving head coach of the Jaguars, coaching the franchise between {{nfly|2003}} and {{nfly|2011}}]]Hired to replace Coughlin was [[Jack Del Rio]]. He was hired on January 16, 2003{{cite web |last=Mortensen |first=Chris |author-link=Chris Mortensen |last2=Pasquarelli |first2=Len |author-link2=Len Pasquarelli |date=January 16, 2003 |title=Coordinator Del Rio replaces Coughlin in Jax |url=https://www.espn.com/espn/print?id=1493632&type=news |access-date=March 18, 2008 |publisher=ESPN}} and fired on November 29, 2011. He was replaced on an interim basis with [[Mel Tucker]].{{cite news|work=NFL.com|publisher=[[National Football League]]|url=https://www.nfl.com/news/jaguars-fire-coach-del-rio-after-eight-plus-seasons-09000d5d82493c0b|title=Jaguars fire coach Del Rio after eight-plus seasons|date=November 29, 2011|access-date=November 29, 2011}} Del Rio coached the team from 2003 to 2011, recording a winning percentage of 48.9% from 139 regular season games, but was fired after going over eight full seasons without winning a division title. ===Mike Mularkey era (2012)=== In 2012, [[Mike Mularkey]] was hired as head coach. His team was hit by several key injuries throughout the season and managed going on 2–14, the worst record in franchise history at the time. As a result, new owner Shahid Khan decided he wanted new leadership and fired the General Manager (GM), Gene Smith. The new GM, Dave Caldwell, decided to fire Mike Mularkey. ===Gus Bradley era (2013–2016)=== In [[2013 Jacksonville Jaguars season|2013]], the Jaguars hired [[Gus Bradley]] to become their new head coach. Bradley served a total of four years as the head coach of the Jaguars ({{nfly|2013}}–{{nfly|2016}}), but never was able to make the playoffs. Bradley was fired shortly before the end of the [[2016 Jacksonville Jaguars season|2016 season]], holding a record of 14–48. ===Doug Marrone era (2016–2020)=== [[Doug Marrone]] replaced Bradley for the final two remaining games of the 2016 season as the interim head coach of the Jaguars. On January 9, 2017, the Jaguars removed the interim tag and hired Marrone to be their fifth head coach. In Marrone's first season as head coach, the Jaguars finished with a 10–6 regular season record in [[2017 Jacksonville Jaguars season|2017]], made the playoffs for the first time since [[2007 Jacksonville Jaguars season|2007]] and made an [[2017-18 NFL playoffs|AFC Conference Championship]] appearance but were defeated by the [[2017 New England Patriots season|New England Patriots]]. Over the next three seasons under Marrone, the Jaguars finished 4th in the [[AFC South]] with losing seasons and failed to make the playoffs for three consecutive seasons. In [[2020 Jacksonville Jaguars season|2020]], Marrone led the Jaguars to a franchise-worst and league worst 1–15 season record in 2020, clinching the Jaguars their first franchise [[List of first overall National Football League draft picks|1st overall pick]] in the [[2021 NFL draft]]. Following the end of the 2020 season, Marrone was fired on January 4, 2021. ===Urban Meyer year (2021)=== On January 14, 2021, [[Urban Meyer]] was hired as the sixth head coach of the Jaguars, replacing [[Doug Marrone]]. On February 10, 2021, Meyer hired [[Chris Doyle (American football)|Chris Doyle]] out of [[University of Iowa]] to become the Jaguars director of sports performance. He was accused of racially motivated actions against Iowa players, which eventually led to a subpoena of both Meyer and then-GM [[Trent Baalke]]. Doyle resigned 2 days later. On September 12, 2021, Meyer began his NFL coaching debut with the Jaguars losing to the [[2021 Houston Texans season|Houston Texans]] by a score of 21–37. On October 1, 2021, Meyer was publicly reprimanded by Jaguars owner [[Shad Khan]] for his behavior seen on social media videos that occurred at his Columbus-area restaurant, Urban Meyer's Pint House. Meyer was seen on video in a bar with a young woman who is not his wife, and appeared to grope the woman. The events took place a day after a loss to the [[Cincinnati Bengals]], dropping the Jaguars' record to 0–4 on the season. He apologized to the team and personnel.{{cite web | url=https://www.espn.com/nfl/story/_/id/32342897/jacksonville-jaguars-owner-shad-khan-urban-meyer-regain-our-trust-respect-inexcusable-conduct | title=Jacksonville Jaguars owner Shad Khan: Urban Meyer 'must regain our trust and respect' after 'inexcusable' conduct | website=ESPN.com | last=DiRocco | first=Michael | date=October 5, 2021 | access-date=October 5, 2021 | language=en}} On October 17, 2021, the Jaguars won against the [[2021 Miami Dolphins season|Miami Dolphins]] by a score of 23–20, earning Meyer his 1st career win as an NFL head coach. The win snapped the Jaguars' 20-game losing streak that dated back to September 13, 2020 – week 1 of the [[2020 NFL season]]. On November 7, 2021, Meyer and the Jaguars defeated the heavily favored [[2021 Buffalo Bills season|Buffalo Bills]] by a score of 9–6. Toward the end of the 2021 season, reports surfaced of Jaguars players and coaches being critical of Meyer's treatment of them, to which the team responded with assertions of his job security.{{Cite web|title=Report: Jaguars players, coaches fed up with Urban Meyer on and off field|url=https://sports.yahoo.com/nfl-jaguars-players-coaches-fed-up-with-urban-meyer-on-and-off-field-173919802.html|access-date=December 12, 2021|website=sports.yahoo.com|language=en-US}}{{Cite web|date=December 11, 2021|title=Sources: Jags plan to keep Urban amid tension|url=https://www.espn.com/nfl/story/_/id/32845194/urban-meyer-jacksonville-jaguars-plans-building-tension-sources-say|access-date=December 12, 2021|website=ESPN.com|language=en}} On December 12, 2021, Meyer lost 20–0 to the [[Tennessee Titans]], giving the Jaguars their 1st losing shutout since 2009 at the time. On December 15, 2021, Meyer was accused of kicking former kicker [[Josh Lambo]] during a pregame warmup on August 19, 2021, against the [[Dallas Cowboys]]. On December 16, 2021, Meyer was fired by the Jaguars and the offensive coordinator, [[Darrell Bevell]], was named Meyer's replacement as head coach on an interim basis.{{Cite web|title=A Statement from Shad Khan|url=https://www.jaguars.com/news/a-statement-from-shad-khan|website=Jaguars.com|access-date=December 16, 2021|archive-date=December 16, 2021|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20211216055506/https://www.jaguars.com/news/a-statement-from-shad-khan|url-status=dead}} Meyer finished his tenure in Jacksonville with a 2–11 (.154) record. ===Doug Pederson era (2022–2024)=== On February 3, 2022, [[Doug Pederson]] was hired as the seventh permanent head coach and ninth overall head coach of the Jaguars, replacing [[Urban Meyer]].{{Cite web|first=John|last=Oehser|title=Official: Pederson hired as head coach|url=https://www.jaguars.com/news/official-pederson-hired-as-head-coach|publisher=[[Jacksonville Jaguars]]|date=February 3, 2022|access-date=February 3, 2022|archive-date=February 4, Pederson as their new head coach|url=https://www.nfl.com/news/jaguars-hiring-doug-pederson-as-new-head-coach|website=NFL.com|date=February 3, 2022|access-date=February 3, 2022}} On January 6, 2025, Doug Pederson was fired by the Jaguars.{{Cite web |date=2025-01-06 |title=Jaguars fire HC Pederson, retain GM Baalke |url=https://www.espn.com/nfl/story/_/id/43308698/jaguars-fire-head-coach-doug-pederson-4-13-season |access-date=2025-01-06 |website=ESPN.com |language=en}} ===Liam Coen era (2025–present)=== On
