Las Vegas Raiders:
{{Short description|None}} {{Use mdy dates|date=June 2024}} [[Image:John Madden in 2007.jpg|thumb|200px|right|[[John Madden]] was the head coach of the Oakland Raiders for ten seasons, from 1969 to 1978.]] The [[Las Vegas Raiders]] are a professional [[American football]] team based in the [[Las Vegas Valley]]. The Raiders compete in the [[National Football League]] (NFL) as a member club of the league's [[American Football Conference]] (AFC) [[AFC West|West division]]. Their stadium is located in [[Paradise, Nevada]]. There have been 25 head coaches in Oakland, Los Angeles, and Las Vegas franchise history. Their current coach is [[Klint Kubiak]], who replaced [[Pete Carroll]] after he coached the team for one season. The Raiders franchise was founded in [[Oakland, California]] in 1959 and became the eighth member of the [[American Football League]] (AFL) in 1960 as a replacement for the [[Minnesota Vikings]], who had moved to the NFL. The Raiders joined the NFL in 1970, after the [[AFL–NFL merger]]. They played in [[Los Angeles]] between 1982 and 1995, before returning to Oakland.{{cite web|url=http://www.profootballhof.com/history/team.jsp?franchise_id=23|title=Oakland Raiders|publisher=Pro Football Hall of Fame|access-date=April 15, 2008|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20061230070530/http://www.profootballhof.com/history/team.jsp?franchise_id=23|archive-date=December 30, 2006|url-status=dead}} As of the end of the [[2023 NFL season|2023 season]], the Raiders have played 983 games in a total of 64 seasons in the AFL and NFL.{{cite web|url=https://www.pro-football-reference.com/teams/rai/|title=Oakland Raiders Franchise Encyclopedia|publisher=pro-football-reference.com|access-date=April 15, 2008|archive-date=July 7, 2017|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170707115845/http://www.pro-football-reference.com/teams/rai/|url-status=live}} In those games, two coaches have won the [[Super Bowl]] with the team: [[John Madden]] in [[Super Bowl XI|1976]] and [[Tom Flores]] in [[Super Bowl XV|1980]] and [[Super Bowl XVIII|1983]]. One coach, [[John Rauch]] in [[AFL Championship#1967 Championship|1966]], won the [[AFL Championship]].{{cite web |url=https://www.pro-football-reference.com/coaches/RaucJo0.htm |title=John Rauch |access-date=May 26, 2008 |publisher=pro-football-reference.com |archive-date=April 8, 2019 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20190408161323/https://www.pro-football-reference.com/coaches/RaucJo0.htm |url-status=live }} Five other coaches, [[Art Shell]], [[Jack Del Rio]], [[Jon Gruden]], [[Bill Callahan (coach)|Bill Callahan]], and [[Rich Bisaccia]], have also taken the Raiders to the playoffs.{{cite web | publisher=NFLTeamHistory.com | url=http://www.nflteamhistory.com/nfl_teams/oakland_raiders/coaching_history.html | title=Oakland Raiders Coaching History | access-date=April 15, 2008 | archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20080517084954/http://www.nflteamhistory.com/nfl_teams/oakland_raiders/coaching_history.html | archive-date=May 17, 2008 | url-status=dead }} Callahan led the Raiders to the Super Bowl. He did this in his first year as head coach of the team.{{cite web |url=https://www.pro-football-reference.com/coaches/CallBi0.htm |title=Bill Callahan |access-date=May 26, 2008 |publisher=pro-football-reference.com |archive-date=January 26, 2013 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130126201925/http://www.pro-football-reference.com/coaches/CallBi0.htm |url-status=live }} Shell and Gruden are the only coaches to have more than one tenure with the team, and Flores and Shell are the only coaches to have coached the team in both Oakland and Los Angeles. Gruden is the only coach to have coached the team in both Oakland and Las Vegas. Rauch is statistically the best, with a winning percentage of .805. However, the all-time leader in both games coached and wins is Madden, with 142 and 103 respectively. Of the 23 Raiders coaches, [[Al Davis]], Madden, and Flores are the only Raider coaches to be inducted into the [[Pro Football Hall of Fame]] for their contributions as coaches. Davis, who was also the Managing General partner and an AFL Commissioner, was in the Hall of Fame class of 1992, Madden was in the 2006 class, and Flores was in the 2021 class. Two coaches, Flores and Shell, are also former players for the Raiders. Shell was also inducted into the Hall of Fame in 1989 as a player.{{cite web | title=Pro Football Hall of Fame – Art Shell | publisher=Pro Football Hall of Fame | url=http://www.profootballhof.com/hof/member.jsp?player_id=193 | access-date=June 3, 2008 | archive-date=June 12, 2008 | Los Angeles Chargers:
{{Short description|None}} {{Use mdy dates|date=June 2024}} The [[Los Angeles Chargers]] are a professional [[American football]] team based in the [[Greater Los Angeles]] area. The Chargers compete in the [[National Football League]] (NFL) as a member club of the league's [[American Football Conference]] (AFC) [[AFC West|West division]]. Their stadium is located in [[Inglewood, California]]. There have been 18 coaches in San Diego and Los Angeles franchise history, including [[Sid Gillman]], who coached the Los Angeles Chargers' first and only season in 1960 before the team's move to [[San Diego]] in 1961. The current head coach is [[Jim Harbaugh]], who was hired on January 24, 2024. Harbaugh replaced [[Giff Smith]], who served as interim head coach after the firing of [[Brandon Staley]] in the [[2023 Los Angeles Chargers season|2023 season]]. ==History== {{Summarize section|date=April 2020}} [[Sid Gillman]] coached the [[San Diego Chargers|Los Angeles and San Diego Chargers]] to five Western Division titles and one league championship in the first six years of the league's existence. His greatest coaching success came after he was persuaded by [[Barron Hilton]], then the Chargers' majority owner, to become the head coach of the American Football League franchise he planned to operate in Los Angeles. When the team's [[general manager]], [[Frank Leahy]], became ill during the Chargers' founding season, Gillman took on additional responsibilities as general manager. As the first coach of the Chargers, Gillman gave the team a personality that matched his own. Gillman's concepts formed the foundation of the so-called "[[West Coast offense]]" that pro football teams are still using.{{cite news |title = Gillman helped engineer West Coast offense |url = https://www.espn.com/classic/obit/s/2003/0103/1486725.html |publisher = [[Associated Press]] |date = January 7, 2003 |access-date = September 7, 2006 |archive-date = August 27, 2007 |archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20070827214913/http://espn.go.com/classic/obit/s/2003/0103/1486725.html |url-status = live }}{{cite news |first = Paul |last = Zimmerman |title = The ''real'' West Coast offense |url = http://sportsillustrated.cnn.com/inside_game/dr_z/news/1999/10/28/inside_football/ |publisher = [[Sports Illustrated]] |date = October 29, 1999 |access-date = September 7, 2006 |archive-date = December 12, 2005 |archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20051212044535/http://sportsillustrated.cnn.com/inside_game/dr_z/news/1999/10/28/inside_football/ |url-status = dead }} He coached the [[San Diego Chargers|Los Angeles and San Diego Chargers]] to five Western Division titles and one league championship in the first six years of the league's existence. He played [[college football]] at [[Ohio State University]] under legendary coach [[Francis Schmidt|Francis "Shut the Gates of Mercy" Schmidt]], forming the basis of his "[[West Coast offense]]."{{cite news | last = Peterson | first = Bill | title = Cincinnati's Connection to Football's "West Coast Offense" | publisher = City Beat | date = August 16, 2006 | url = http://www.citybeat.com/2006-08-16/sports.shtml | access-date = September 7, 2006 | archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20070128151352/http://www.citybeat.com/2006-08-16/sports.shtml | archive-date = January 28, 2007 | url-status = dead }} The term "West Coast Offense," as it is now commonly used, derives from a 1993 [[Bernie Kosar]] quote, publicized by ''[[Sports Illustrated]]'' writer [[Paul Lionel Zimmerman|Paul Zimmerman]] (or "Dr. Z"). Originally the term referred to the "[[Air Coryell]]" system used by two west coast teams beginning in the 1970s, the [[San Diego Chargers]] and [[Oakland Raiders]]. However, a reporter mistakenly applied Kosar's quote about the Air Coryell system to the 1980s-era attack of Walsh's [[San Francisco 49ers]].[http://sportsillustrated.cnn.com/inside_game/dr_z/news/1999/10/28/inside_football/ 1999 SportsIllustrated.com article] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20051212044535/http://sportsillustrated.cnn.com/inside_game/dr_z/news/1999/10/28/inside_football/ |date=December 12, 2005 }}. Retrieved May 20, 2005. Initially, Walsh resisted having the term misapplied to his own distinct system, but the moniker stuck. Now the term is also commonly used to refer to pass-offenses that may not be closely related to either the Air Coryell system or Walsh's pass-strategy. Don Coryell coached the [[San Diego Chargers]] from 1978 to 1986. He is well known for his innovations to football's passing offense. Coryell's offense today is commonly known as [[American football strategy#Offensive nomenclature|"Air Coryell"]]. However, the Charger offense lacked the ability to control the clock, resulting in their defense spending too much time on the field. As a result, they fell short of getting to the [[Super Bowl]]. He was inducted into the [[Los Angeles Chargers Hall of Fame|Chargers Hall of Fame]] in 1986. Coryell is a member of the [[College Football Hall of Fame]]. He did not use a playbook. [[Al Saunders]] was the coach for the Chargers from 1986 to 1988 and became a citizen of the United States in 1960, one of the four foreign-born coaches in the NFL. In college played [[Defensive back]] and [[Wide receiver]] for the Spartans of [[San Jose State University|San Jose State University (SJSU)]] from 1966 to 1968 where he was a three-year starter, team captain, and an Academic All-American. In the 1970s, Al Saunders joined the coaching staff at [[University of Southern California|USC]] and [[San Diego State University|San Diego State University (SDSU)]], whose [[SDSU Aztecs]] were then under the control of [[Head Coach]] [[Don Coryell]]. Saunders would go with Coryell to NFL when Coryell became the head coach of the [[San Diego Chargers]]. Statistics correct as of December 30, 2007, after the end of the [[2007 NFL season]]. [[Bobby Ross]] coached the Chargers from 1992 to 1996, and is the only coach to win awards while coaching the Chargers. In 1992, Ross won the [[Pro Football Weekly NFL Coach of the Year]], the [[Maxwell Football Club NFL Coach of the Year]] and the [[UPI NFL Coach of the Year]]. The [[Pro Football Weekly NFL Coach of the Year]] is presented annually by various news and sports organizations to the [[National Football League]] (NFL) head coach who has done the most outstanding job of working with the talent he has at his disposal. The [[Maxwell Football Club NFL Coach of the Year]] was created in 1989 and is originally titled the [[Earle "Greasy" Neale]] Award for Professional Coach of the Year. The [[United Press International]] (UPI) NFL Coach of the Year award was first presented in 1955. Before the [[AFL-NFL merger]], an award was also given to the most outstanding coach from the [[American Football League|AFL]]. When the leagues merged in 1970, separate awards were given to the best coaches from the [[American Football Conference|AFC]] and [[National Football Conference|NFC]] conferences. The [[United Press International|UPI]] discontinued the awards after 1996. The [[San Diego Chargers]] hired Schottenheimer as their 13th head coach on January 29, 2002. Schottenheimer posted a 47–33 record (.588) with the Chargers. His success did not come immediately, as the team posted a 4–12 record in 2003, thereby "earning" the first overall pick in the draft (this was the last time that a team with the worst record in the NFL kept its head coach the following season, even considering the three other 4–12 teams that season replaced their head coaches, Oakland, Arizona, and the New York Giants hiring Norv Turner, Dennis Green, and Tom Coughlin, respectively).{{cite web |url=https://www.espn.com/nfl/news/story?id=2763552 |title=Chargers head coach Schottenheimer fired |access-date=April 30, 2008 |work=ESPN |date=February 13, 2007 |archive-date=June 2, 2008 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20080602095536/http://sports.espn.go.com/nfl/news/story?id=2763552 |url-status=live }} He was named NFL Coach Of The Year for the [[2004 NFL season]]. Schottenheimer led the team to two playoff appearances, his 17th and 18th as a head coach. However, both appearances resulted in disappointing losses to the underdog [[New York Jets]] in overtime in [[NFL playoffs, 2004-05|2005]] and the [[New England Patriots]] in [[NFL playoffs, 2006-07|2007]], bringing his playoff record to 5–13. Schottenheimer was abruptly fired by San Diego on February 12, 2007. Schottenheimer was fired because of a strained relationship with general manager [[A. J. Smith]], which reached a breaking point when four assistants ([[Cam Cameron]], [[Wade Phillips]], [[Rob Chudzinski]] and [[Greg Manusky]]) left for positions with other teams. [[Bobby Ross]] holds the best record percentage wise in the playoffs. [[Norv Turner]] holds the best regular season coaching record, with 0.640, followed by [[Pro Football Hall of Fame|Hall of Famer]] Sid Gillman with 0.608. Tennessee Titans:
{{Short description|None}} {{Use mdy dates|date=June 2024}} [[File:LP Field 2009 crop.jpg|thumb|right|The Titans have played in [[Nissan Stadium (Nashville)|Nissan Stadium]] (formerly LP Field) since 1999.]] The [[Tennessee Titans]], previously known as the [[Houston Oilers]] and Tennessee Oilers, are a professional [[American football]] team based in [[Nashville, Tennessee|Nashville]], [[Tennessee]]. They are a member of the [[AFC South|South]] division of the [[American Football Conference]] (AFC) in the [[National Football League]] (NFL). The Tennessee Titans have had 20 head coaches in its franchise history. As the Houston Oilers based in [[Houston]], [[Texas]], the team began playing in 1960 as a charter member of the [[American Football League]] (AFL). The Oilers won two [[AFL championship]]s before joining the NFL as part of the [[AFL-NFL merger]]. The team relocated to Tennessee in 1997 and played in [[Memphis, Tennessee|Memphis]] for one season before moving to Nashville. For two seasons, the team was known as the Tennessee Oilers before changing its name to the Titans in 1999.{{cite web|url=http://www.titansonline.com/history/ |title=History |access-date=May 25, 2008 |publisher=Tennessee Titans |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20080111142618/http://www.titansonline.com/history/ |archive-date=January 11, 2008 |url-status=dead }} Head coach [[Jeff Fisher]] led the Titans to their only [[Super Bowl]] appearance in [[Super Bowl XXXIV]] following the [[1999 Tennessee Titans season|1999 season]]. He continued his success with playoff appearances in [[2000 Tennessee Titans season|2000]], [[2002 Tennessee Titans season|2002]], and [[2003 Tennessee Titans season|2003]]. His coaching career began to decline and, from 2004 to 2010, his only playoff appearances included [[2007 Tennessee Titans season|2007]] and [[2008 Tennessee Titans season|2008]]. In January 2011, it was announced that Fisher and the Titans had mutually parted ways. Fisher's tenure at head coach from 1994 to 2010 included six playoff appearances, one [[AFC Championship]] appearance, and one Super Bowl appearance. His 142 wins and 120 losses are each a franchise record for a Titans head coach. Following Fisher, the Titans promoted former player and longtime offensive line coach [[Mike Munchak]] to head coach. He missed the playoffs from 2011 to 2013 before being fired in January 2014. [[Ken Whisenhunt]] became head coach shortly after, and tied a franchise worst 2–14 record in [[2014 Tennessee Titans season|2014]]. [[Mike Mularkey]], who was originally hired as tight ends coach in 2014 and promoted to assistant head coach in 2015, replaced Whisenhunt on an interim basis after a 1–6 start in [[2015 Tennessee Titans season|2015]]. Mularkey was retained as head coach and led the Titans to two 9–7 seasons, including a playoff appearance in [[2017 Tennessee Titans season|2017]]. Mularkey and the Titans agreed to part ways in January 2018. Former NFL player and coach, [[Mike Vrabel]], was hired as head coach soon after. In his first four seasons, Vrabel led the Titans to an AFC Championship appearance in [[2019 Tennessee Titans season|2019]], back-to-back AFC South division titles in [[2020 Tennessee Titans season|2020]] and [[2021 Tennessee Titans season|2021]], and earned the #1 seed for the AFC in the [[2021–22 NFL playoffs]]. For his team's performance in 2021, Vrabel was named that season's [[National Football League Coach of the Year Award|NFL Coach of the Year]]. After back-to-back losing seasons in [[2022 Tennessee Titans season|2022]] and [[2023 Tennessee Titans season|2023]], Vrabel was fired by the Titans in January 2024. Shortly afterwards, former [[Cincinnati Bengals]] offensive coordinator [[Brian Callahan]] was hired as the franchise's 20th head coach. After going 3–14 in [[2024 Tennessee Titans season|2024]], and beginning the season 1–5 in [[2025 Tennessee Titans season|2025]], Callahan was fired in October of that year. The Titans then named senior offensive assistant [[Mike McCoy (American football coach)|Mike McCoy]], who had been a head coach for the [[San Diego Chargers]] earlier in his career, as their new interim head coach. The current head coach of the Titans is [[Robert Saleh]].{{cite web|url=https://www.tennesseetitans.com/news/it-s-official-titans-hire-robert-saleh-as-team-s-new-head-coach|work=[[Tennessee Titans]]|title=It's Official: Titans Hire Robert Saleh as Team's New Head Coach|date=January 22, 2026|first=Jim|last=Wyatt|access-date=January 23, 2026}} ==Key== {| class="wikitable" |- | '''#''' | Number of coaches{{ref label|Note1|a|a}} |- | '''GC''' | Games coached |- | '''W''' | Wins |- | '''L''' | Losses |- | '''T''' | Ties |- | '''Win%''' | Winning percentage |- |style="background-color:#FFE6BD"| {{0|12}}† | Elected into the [[Pro Football Hall of Fame]] as a coach |- |style="background-color:#E4D00A"| {{0|12}}‡ | Elected into the [[Pro Football Hall of Fame]] as a player |- |style="background-color:#ddffdd"| {{0|12}}* | Spent entire NFL head coaching career with the Oilers/Titans |} ==Coaches== ''Note: Statistics are correct through the end of the [[2025 NFL season]].'' {| class="wikitable" border="1" style="text-align:center;" |- ! rowspan="2" | # ! rowspan="2" | Image ! rowspan="2" scope="col" width="200" | Name ! rowspan="2" scope="col" width="150" | Term {{ref label|Note1|b|b}} ! colspan="5" | Regular season ! colspan="4" | Playoffs ! rowspan="2" | Achievements ! rowspan="2" | Reference |- ! Denver Broncos:
{{Short description|None}} {{Use mdy dates|date=June 2024}} [[Image:DSCN5753 invescofieldatmilehigh e.jpg|250px|thumb|right|[[Empower Field at Mile High]] (formerly Invesco Field at Mile High) has been the home of the Broncos since 2001.]] The [[Denver Broncos]] are a professional [[American football]] franchise based in [[Denver]], [[Colorado]]. They are members of the [[AFC West|West Division]] of the [[American Football Conference]] (AFC) in the [[National Football League]] (NFL). The team began playing in [[1960 AFL season|1960]] as a charter member of the [[American Football League]] (AFL), and joined the NFL as part of the [[AFL-NFL merger]]. The team has played their home games at [[Empower Field at Mile High]] since {{nfly|2001}}.{{cite web|url=http://www.sportsauthorityfieldatmilehigh.com/stadium-information/about-us/event-history|title=Event History|work=Sports Authority Field at Mile High|access-date=January 19, 2015|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20150119230338/http://www.sportsauthorityfieldatmilehigh.com/stadium-information/about-us/event-history|archive-date=January 19, 2015|url-status=dead}} There have been 20 [[head coach]]es for the Broncos franchise as of 2023. The franchise's first head coach was [[Frank Filchock]], who coached until {{nfly|1961}}. [[Mike Shanahan]] is the franchise's all-time leader for the most regular season games coached (208), the most regular season game wins (130), and the most playoff game wins (8). Shanahan and [[Dan Reeves]], are tied for the most playoff games coached (13). Shanahan was the first Broncos head coach to win a [[Super Bowl]] following the 1997 season, and repeated the feat following the 1998 season. The Broncos next Super Bowl victory was for [[Super Bowl 50]] following the 2015 season under the leadership of coach [[Gary Kubiak]] who had previously played for Denver and served as an assistant coach. [[Jack Faulkner]], [[John Ralston (coach)|John Ralston]], [[Red Miller]], and Reeves have been named the [[United Press International]] (UPI) [[National Football League Coach of the Year Award|NFL Coach of the Year]], at least once with the Broncos. Filchock, Faulkner, [[Mac Speedie]], [[Jerry Smith (American football coach)|Jerry Smith]], Ralston, and Miller spent their entire coaching careers with the Broncos. Speedie, [[Ray Malavasi]], Miller, Shanahan, and Kubiak had been assistant coaches with the Broncos before they became head coaches with the Broncos.{{cite web|url=http://www.denverbroncos.com/resources/custom/PDF/RecordBook/2008/AllTimeCoaches.pdf|title=All-Time Coaches Roster|publisher=NFL Enterprises LLC|work=[[Denver Broncos]]|access-date=November 11, 2008}} {{Dead link|date=October 2010|bot=H3llBot}} The most recent head coach of the Broncos was [[Sean Payton]], who was hired on January 31, 2023. __TOC__ ==Key== {| class="wikitable" |- |'''#''' |Number of coachesA running total of the number of coaches of the Broncos. Kansas City Chiefs:
{{Short description|None}} {{Use mdy dates|date=June 2024}} [[File:Andy reid 2018 (cropped).jpg|right|thumb|200px|Current Chiefs head coach [[Andy Reid]]]] The [[Kansas City Chiefs]] of the [[National Football League]] (NFL) have had 13 head coaches in their franchise history. The franchise was founded in 1960 by [[Lamar Hunt]] and were known as the Dallas Texans when the team was located in [[Dallas, Texas]].{{cite web|title=Kansas City Chiefs History–1960s |work=Kansas City Chiefs official website |url=http://www.kcchiefs.com/history/60s/ |access-date=July 9, 2008 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20080609053609/http://www.kcchiefs.com/history/60s/ |archive-date=June 9, 2008 |url-status=dead }} The team relocated to [[Kansas City, Missouri]] and were renamed the Chiefs in 1963. The franchise was a charter member of the [[American Football League]] (AFL) before entering into the NFL following the [[AFL-NFL merger]].{{cite web|title=Kansas City Chiefs History–1970s |work=Kansas City Chiefs official website |url=http://www.kcchiefs.com/history/70s/ |access-date=July 9, 2008 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20080609052751/http://www.kcchiefs.com/history/70s/ |archive-date=June 9, 2008 |url-status=dead }} [[Hank Stram]], the team's first head coach, led the Chiefs to three AFL championship victories and two appearances in the [[Super Bowl]]. Stram was the team's longest-tenured head coach, holding the position from 1960 to 1974.{{cite web|title=Kansas City Chiefs History–1980s |work=Kansas City Chiefs official website |url=http://www.kcchiefs.com/history/80s/ |access-date=July 9, 2008 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20080501122552/http://www.kcchiefs.com/history/80s/ |archive-date=May 1, 2008 |url-status=dead }}{{cite web|title=Kansas City Chiefs History–1990s |work=Kansas City Chiefs official website |url=http://www.kcchiefs.com/history/90s/ |access-date=July 9, 2008 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20080610154419/http://www.kcchiefs.com/history/90s/ |archive-date=June 10, 2008 |url-status=dead }} [[Marty Schottenheimer]] was hired in 1989 and led Kansas City to seven playoff appearances in his ten seasons as head coach. Of the thirteen Chiefs coaches, Hank Stram, [[Marv Levy]], and [[Dick Vermeil]] have been elected into the [[Pro Football Hall of Fame]].{{cite web|title=Hall of Famers by Franchise |work=Pro Football Hall of Fame Official website |url=http://www.profootballhof.com/hof/teams.html |access-date=July 9, 2008 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20080707032032/http://www.profootballhof.com/hof/teams.html |archive-date=July 7, 2008 |url-status=dead }} Seven head coaches have led the Chiefs to the playoffs, of those seven, only three won a game in the playoffs. [[Romeo Crennel]], who coached the team for three games in 2011 and the entire 2012 season, is the team's shortest tenured head coach. He was fired following what was statistically the worst season in franchise history in 2012. [[Andy Reid]] has been the head coach since 2013. Following the Chiefs' [[Super Bowl LIV|Super Bowl championship]] in the 2019 season, Reid became the franchise leader in playoff wins. ==Key== {| class="wikitable" |- |'''#''' |Number of coachesA running total of the number of coaches of the Texans/Chiefs. {{Short description|None}} {{Use mdy dates|date=June 2024}} [[File:Mike Ditka.jpg|thumb|upright|[[Mike Ditka]] was the head coach of the Bears from [[1982 Chicago Bears season|1982]] to [[1992 Chicago Bears season|1992]] and was elected into the [[Pro Football Hall of Fame]] in [[1988 in sports|1988]].{{cite web|url=https://www.pro-football-reference.com/coaches/DitkMi0.htm|title=Mike Ditka's Coaching Record|publisher=Pro Football Reference|access-date=March 18, 2008}}]] There have been 18 head coaches for the [[Chicago Bears]], including coaches for the Decatur Staleys (1919–1920) and Chicago Staleys (1921). The Bears franchise was founded as the [[Decatur, Illinois|Decatur]] Staleys, a charter member of the American Professional Football Association. The team moved to [[Chicago, Illinois|Chicago]] in 1921, and changed its name to the Bears in 1922, the same year the American Professional Football Association (APFA) changed its name to the National Football League (NFL). The Chicago Bears have played more than 1,000 games. Of those games, five different coaches have won [[History of National Football League Championship|NFL championships]] with the team: [[George Halas]] in {{nfly|1921}}, [[NFL Championship Game, 1933|1933]], [[NFL Championship Game, 1940|1940]], [[NFL Championship Game, 1941|1941]], [[NFL Championship Game, 1946|1946]] and [[NFL Championship Game, 1963|1963]]; [[Ralph Jones]] in [[NFL Championship Game, 1932|1932]]; [[Heartley Anderson|Hunk Anderson]] and [[Luke Johnsos]] in [[NFL Championship Game, 1943|1943]]; and [[Mike Ditka]] in [[Super Bowl XX|1985]].{{cite web | work=NFLTeamHistory.com | url=http://www.nflteamhistory.com/nfl_teams/chicago_bears/championship_history.html | title=Chicago Bears Championship History | access-date=March 18, 2008 | archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20080517201444/http://www.nflteamhistory.com/nfl_teams/chicago_bears/championship_history.html | archive-date=May 17, 2008 | url-status=dead }} George Halas is the only coach to have more than one tenure and is the all-time leader in games coached and games won, while Ralph Jones leads all coaches in winning percentage with .706.{{cite web|url=https://www.pro-football-reference.com/coaches/JoneRa0.htm|title=Ralph Jones' Coaching Record|publisher=Pro Football Reference|access-date=March 18, 2008|archive-date=January 20, 2011|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110120174804/http://www.pro-football-reference.com/coaches/JoneRa0.htm|url-status=live}} [[Abe Gibron]] is statistically the worst coach of the Bears in terms of winning percentage, with a .268 average.{{cite web|url=https://www.pro-football-reference.com/coaches/GibrAb0.htm|title=Abe Gibron's Coaching Record|publisher=Pro Football Reference|access-date=March 18, 2008|archive-date=October 2, 2011|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20111002191341/http://www.pro-football-reference.com/coaches/GibrAb0.htm|url-status=live}} Of the 18 Bears coaches, three have been elected to the [[Pro Football Hall of Fame]]: George Halas, [[Paddy Driscoll]], and Mike Ditka.{{cite web | title=Hall of Famers by Franchise | work=[[Pro Football Hall of Fame]] | url=http://www.profootballhof.com/hof/teams.aspx | access-date=March 18, 2008 | archive-date=October 15, 2015 | archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20151015213446/http://www.profootballhof.com/hof/teams.aspx | url-status=live }} Several former players have been head coach for the Bears, including George Halas, [[Heartley Anderson|Hunk Anderson]], Luke Johnsos, Paddy Driscoll, [[Jim Dooley]], [[Abe Gibron]] and Mike Ditka. After Ditka was fired following the 1992 season, the Bears went through six head coaches starting with [[Dave Wannstedt]], who coached until 1998. [[Dick Jauron]] took over in 1999 until he was fired in 2003. [[Lovie Smith]] was hired on January 14, 2004.{{cite news | work=Yahoo Sports | url=https://www.usatoday.com/sports/football/nfl/bears/2004-01-14-smith-bears_x.htm | title=Bears hire Smith to be head coach | access-date=March 18, 2008 | date=January 16, 2004}} Smith was fired on December 31, 2012, after the Bears missed the playoffs with a 10–6 record after starting the season 7–1.{{cite news|url=http://www.chicagotribune.com/sports/football/bears/chi-chicago-bears-fire-coach-lovie-smith-2012,0,1783180.story|title=Lovie Smith out after nine seasons as Bears coach|first=Dan|last=Pompei|work=[[Chicago Tribune]]|date=December 31, 2012|access-date=December 31, 2012|archive-date=December 31, 2012|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20121231202354/http://www.chicagotribune.com/sports/football/bears/chi-chicago-bears-fire-coach-lovie-smith-2012,0,1783180.story|url-status=live}} On January 16, 2013, [[Marc Trestman]] was hired to be the new head coach to take Smith's place.{{cite news| url=http://articles.chicagotribune.com/2013-01-16/sports/chi-bears-hire-marc-trestman-20130115_1_head-coach-marc-trestman-coach-trestman| title=Trestman chosen to be bears new head coach| work=[[Chicago Tribune]]| first=Brad| last=Biggs| date=January 16, 2013| access-date=December 6, 2014| archive-date=December 25, 2014| archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20141225012319/http://articles.chicagotribune.com/2013-01-16/sports/chi-bears-hire-marc-trestman-20130115_1_head-coach-marc-trestman-coach-trestman| url-status=dead}} Trestman was fired on December 29, 2014, with a 13–19 record over two seasons.{{cite news|url=https://www.nfl.com/news/chicago-bears-fire-marc-trestman-0ap3000000451258|title=Chicago Bears fire Marc Trestman|work=[[National Football League]]|publisher=NFL Enterprises LLC|date=December 29, 2014|access-date=December 29, 2014|archive-date=June 21, 2020|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200621163423/https://www.nfl.com/news/chicago-bears-fire-marc-trestman-0ap3000000451258|url-status=live}} On January 16, 2015, [[John Fox (American football)|John Fox]] was hired as the new head coach of the team.{{cite web| title=Bears hire John Fox as new head coach| url=http://www.chicagotribune.com/sports/football/bears/chi-bears-hire-john-fox-coach-story.html| website=Chicago Tribune| date=January 16, 2015| access-date=January 19, 2015| archive-date=January 19, 2015| archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20150119192501/http://www.chicagotribune.com/sports/football/bears/chi-bears-hire-john-fox-coach-story.html| url-status=live}} He compiled a 14–34 record over three seasons before being fired on January 1, 2018.{{cite web|last=Patra|first=Kevin|url=https://www.nfl.com/news/chicago-bears-fire-coach-john-fox-after-5-11-season-0ap3000000901544|title=Chicago Bears fire coach John Fox after 5-11 season|publisher=[[National Football League]]|date=January 1, 2018|access-date=January 1, 2018|archive-date=July 30, 2020|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200730153618/https://www.nfl.com/news/chicago-bears-fire-coach-john-fox-after-5-11-season-0ap3000000901544|url-status=live}} A week later, [[Matt Nagy]] became the new head coach.{{cite news|last=Biggs|first=Brad|url=http://www.chicagotribune.com/sports/football/bears/ct-spt-bears-matt-nagy-coach-20180108-story.html|title=Bears hire Matt Nagy as franchise's 16th head coach|newspaper=[[Chicago Tribune]]|date=January 8, 2018|access-date=January 8, 2018|archive-date=January 8, 2018|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180108231857/http://www.chicagotribune.com/sports/football/bears/ct-spt-bears-matt-nagy-coach-20180108-story.html|url-status=live}} Nagy was fired after the 2021 season. Matt Eberflus was named the new head coach on January 27, 2022. After losing a sixth game in a row on Thanksgiving, November 28, 2024, Eberflus was fired and finished with a .304 winning percentage, the third worst in team history. Thomas Brown was named interim Head Coach for the remaining 5 games of the season. Ben Johnson was named the current head coach of the team on January 21, 2025.{{Cite web |last=Cronin |first=Courtney |date=2025-01-20 |title=Bears name Lions OC Johnson new head coach |url=https://www.espn.com/nfl/story/_/id/43495034/sources-bears-finalizing-deal-hire-lions-ben-johnson-coach |access-date=2025-01-22 |website=[[ESPN]] |language=en}} [[File:GeorgeHalasBaseball.jpg|upright|thumb|right|[[George Halas]], founder of the Bears, shown here during his brief career with the [[New York Yankees]] in [[1919 in sports|1919]]{{cite web|url=https://www.baseball-reference.com/h/halasge01.shtml|title=George Halas' Statistics|publisher=Baseball Reference|access-date=March 20, 2008}}]] ==Key== {| class="wikitable" style="font-size:90%;" |- |'''#''' |Number of coaches |- |'''GC''' |Games Coached |- |'''W''' |Wins |- |'''L''' |Loses |- |'''T''' |Ties |- |'''Win%''' |[[Winning percentage]] |- |'''–''' |Returning from previous year(s) of coaching |- |style="background-color:#FFE6BD"| {{0}}{{0}}† |Elected into the [[Pro Football Hall of Fame]] as a coach |- |style="background-color:#E4D00A"|{{0}}{{0}}‡ |Elected into the [[Pro Football Hall of Fame]] as a player |- |style="background-color:#ddffdd"| {{0}}{{0}}* |Spent entire NFL head coaching career with the Bears |- |style="background-color:#CCCCFF"| {{0}}{{0}}§ |Spent head coaching career in both [[Canadian Football League|CFL]] and NFL |} ==Coaches== :''Note: Statistics are accurate through the end of the [[2024 NFL season]].'' {| class="wikitable" |align="center" colspan="19" style="{{NFLPrimaryStyle|Chicago Bears|border=2}};" | '''Decatur Staleys''' |- ! rowspan="2" | #{{ref label|Note1|a|a}} ! rowspan="2" | Image ! rowspan="2" | Name ! rowspan="2" | Term ! colspan="5" | Regular season ! colspan="3" | Playoffs ! rowspan="2" | Achievements |- ! Staley]] Company of [[Decatur, Illinois|Decatur]], [[Illinois]] in 1919 as a company team. * {{note label|Note1|d|d}} No official records for the 1919 season in Chicago Bears record books, but the team finished the season 6–1. * {{note label|Note1|e|e}} Halas' full coaching record with the Bears is 497 regular season games coached with a record of 318–148–31 and a W–L percentage of .682. Carolina Panthers:
{{Short description|none}} {{Use mdy dates|date=June 2024}} [[File:Ron Rivera (cropped).jpg|150px|thumb|right|[[Ron Rivera]] (left), the fourth and former head coach of the Carolina Panthers, signing autographs with NFL commissioner [[Roger Goodell]]|alt=see caption]] The [[Carolina Panthers]] are a professional [[American football]] club based in [[Charlotte, North Carolina]]. They play in the [[NFC South|southern division]] of the [[National Football Conference]] (NFC), one of the two conferences of the [[National Football League]] (NFL). Since the team began play in 1995, there have been ten (seven full-time and three interim) head coaches.{{cite web|title=Head Coaches|url=http://www.panthers.com/team/history/head-coaches.html|publisher=[[Carolina Panthers]]|accessdate=November 12, 2013|date=November 11, 2013|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20131112030142/http://www.panthers.com/team/history/head-coaches.html|archive-date=November 12, 2013|url-status=dead}} In the NFL, head coaches are responsible for managing the team and setting the game plan; play-calling duties are either made by the head coach or delegated by him to an assistant coach.{{cite web|last=Pompei|first=Dan|title=NFL head coaches have decisions to make on play-calling duties|url=http://articles.chicagotribune.com/2009-02-01/sports/0901310152_1_todd-haley-calling-offensive|work=[[Chicago Tribune]]|access-date=November 13, 2013|date=February 1, 2009|archive-date=November 13, 2013|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20131113182125/http://articles.chicagotribune.com/2009-02-01/sports/0901310152_1_todd-haley-calling-offensive|url-status=dead}} The team's first head coach, [[Dom Capers]], led the team for its first four seasons, recording a regular-season record of 30–34 (.469 winning percentage). in 1996.{{cite web|title=Carolina Panthers Playoff History|url=https://www.pro-football-reference.com/teams/car/playoffs.htm|publisher=[[Pro-Football-Reference.com]]|accessdate=November 12, 2013|archive-date=October 29, 2013|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20131029054852/http://www.pro-football-reference.com/teams/car/playoffs.htm|url-status=live}} Capers was named coach of the year by [[Pro Football Weekly]]/[[Pro Football Writers Association|PFWA]] in 1995 and 1996; he was also awarded coach of the year by several other organizations in 1996, including the [[Associated Press]], the [[Maxwell Football Club]], ''[[Sporting News]]'', and [[United Press International]].{{cite web|title=Honors|url=http://www.panthers.com/team/history/honors.html|publisher=Carolina Panthers|accessdate=November 12, 2013|date=November 11, 2013|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20070927021623/http://www.panthers.com/Team/Default.aspx?id=792|archive-date=September 27, 2007|url-status=dead}} After Capers' dismissal following the 1998 season, the team brought in [[George Seifert]] as their second head coach. Over Seifert's three seasons the team never made the playoffs and the team had a regular-season record of 16–32 (.333 winning percentage). [[John Fox (American football)|John Fox]], the team's third coach, was the longest-tenured coach in team history. In his nine seasons as head coach the Panthers recorded a regular-season record of 73–71 (.507), the most wins for a head coach in team history, and a playoff record of 5–3. The team's fourth head coach, [[Ron Rivera]], served nine seasons as head coach and had a record of 76–63–1 (.546) during his tenure, with a 3–4 record in the playoffs. Rivera has the highest winning percentage of any coach in team history. Rivera led the team to a record four playoff appearances, including three straight division titles. Capers led the team to a playoff appearance in the 1996 season, winning once at home before losing in the [[NFC Championship Game]] to the [[Green Bay Packers]]. Fox led the team to three playoff appearances (2003, 2005, and 2008), winning the NFC Championship in 2003 before losing in [[Super Bowl XXXVIII]] to the [[New England Patriots]] and making the NFC Championship game in 2005 before losing to the [[Seattle Seahawks]]. Rivera led the team to three straight playoff appearances from 2013 to 2015, culminating in a loss in [[Super Bowl 50]]. He returned the team to the playoffs in 2017, losing in the Wild Card round. On January 25, 2024, the team agreed to terms with Buccaneers offensive coordinator Dave Canales as the seventh full-time head coach in franchise history Thursday. "Dave's background is rooted in success," Panthers owner David Tepper said. "He has an innovative mindset and positive energy that connects well with players and staff. We are impressed with his ability to bring out the best in players." ==Key== {| class="wikitable" |- |'''#''' |Number of coachesA running total of the number of coaches of the Panthers. Minnesota Vikings:
{{Short description|None}}
{{Use mdy dates|date=December 2023}}
[[File:Bud Grant 2019 (48977735518) (cropped).jpg|thumb|upright|[[Bud Grant]] was head coach of the Minnesota Vikings from 1967 to 1983 and 1985.|alt=Bud Grant wearing a purple suit and giving a thumbs-up.]]

The [[Minnesota Vikings]] are a professional [[American football]] team based in [[Minneapolis]], [[Minnesota]]. The Vikings are members of the [[NFC North|North Division]] of the [[National Football Conference]] in the [[National Football League]] (NFL). The club was founded by Minneapolis businessmen [[Bill Boyer]], [[H. P. Skoglund]] and [[Max Winter]] in 1959 as a member of the [[American Football League]]. However, they forfeited their membership in January 1960 and became the National Football League's 14th franchise in 1961.<ref>{{cite web |title=Team Facts |url=https://www.profootballhof.com/teams/minnesota-vikings/team-facts/ |website=[[Pro Football Hall of Fame]] |access-date=December 19, 2023 |archive-date=December 19, 2023 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20231219172004/https://www.profootballhof.com/teams/minnesota-vikings/team-facts/ |url-status=live }}</ref>

There have been ten head coaches in the history of the franchise,<ref>{{cite web |title=Minnesota Vikings Team Encyclopedia |url=https://www.pro-football-reference.com/teams/min/ |website=[[Pro-Football-Reference.com]] |publisher=[[Sports Reference]] |access-date=November 26, 2009 |archive-date=January 29, 2018 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180129142034/https://www.pro-football-reference.com/teams/min/ |url-status=live }}</ref> beginning with [[Norm Van Brocklin]], who was head coach for six seasons between [[1961 Minnesota Vikings season|1961]] and [[1967 Minnesota Vikings season|1967]].<ref name="Van Brocklin">{{cite web |title=Norm Van Brocklin |url=https://www.pro-football-reference.com/coaches/VanBNo0.htm |website=[[Pro-Football-Reference.com]] |publisher=[[Sports Reference]] |access-date=November 26, 2009 |archive-date=November 20, 2010 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20101120230722/http://www.pro-football-reference.com/coaches/VanBNo0.htm |url-status=live }}</ref> Van Brocklin's successor, [[Bud Grant]], is the only coach to have had more than one tenure with the franchise, and also the only one to have won an [[History of National Football League Championship|NFL championship]] with the team, at the [[1969 NFL Championship Game]].<ref name="Grant">{{cite web |title=Bud Grant |url=https://www.pro-football-reference.com/coaches/GranBu0.htm |website=[[Pro-Football-Reference.com]] |publisher=[[Sports Reference]] |access-date=November 26, 2009 |archive-date=May 14, 2011 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110514000456/http://www.pro-football-reference.com/coaches/GranBu0.htm |url-status=live }}</ref><ref>{{cite web|title=Minnesota Vikings Championship History |url=http://www.nflteamhistory.com/nfl_teams/minnesota_vikings/championship_history.html |work=NFLTeamHistory.com |access-date=November 26, 2009 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20091204071337/http://nflteamhistory.com/nfl_teams/minnesota_vikings/championship_history.html |archive-date=December 4, 2009 }}</ref> Grant is the all-time leader in games coached (243), wins (151), and [[winning percentage]] ({{winning percentage|158|96|5}}).<ref name="Grant"/> [[Les Steckel]] has the worst winning percentage of the franchise's ten head coaches ({{winning percentage|3|13}}), with just three wins in his only season in charge.<ref name="Steckel">{{cite web |title=Les Steckel |url=https://www.pro-football-reference.com/coaches/StecLe0.htm |website=[[Pro-Football-Reference.com]] |publisher=[[Sports Reference]] |access-date=November 26, 2009 |archive-date=February 14, 2010 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20100214113634/http://www.pro-football-reference.com/coaches/StecLe0.htm |url-status=live }}</ref> Two Vikings coaches have been inducted into the [[Pro Football Hall of Fame]]: Grant and Van Brocklin, although Van Brocklin was elected for his playing career.<ref>{{cite web |title=Players |url=https://www.profootballhof.com/players/?pg=1&team=Minnesota%20Vikings |website=[[Pro Football Hall of Fame]] |access-date=December 19, 2023 |archive-date=December 19, 2023 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20231219172005/https://www.profootballhof.com/players/?pg=1&team=Minnesota%20Vikings |url-status=live }}</ref> [[Mike Tice]] is the only former Vikings player to have become a head coach for the franchise.<ref name="Tice">{{cite web |title=Mike Tice |url=https://www.pro-football-reference.com/coaches/TiceMi0.htm |website=[[Pro-Football-Reference.com]] |publisher=[[Sports Reference]] |access-date=November 26, 2009 |archive-date=September 11, 2010 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20100911095158/http://www.pro-football-reference.com/coaches/TiceMi0.htm |url-status=live }}</ref> Dennis Green was the first African American head coach in franchise history. Former defensive coordinator [[Leslie Frazier]], who took over as interim head coach from [[Brad Childress]] after the latter was fired on November 22, 2010,<ref name="childress_fired">{{cite web |title=Childress fired by Vikings; Frazier named interim head coach |url=https://www.nfl.com/news/childress-fired-by-vikings-frazier-promoted-to-head-coach-09000d5d81c535bf |website=NFL.com |publisher=[[National Football League]] |date=November 22, 2010 |access-date=November 22, 2010 |archive-date=November 25, 2010 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20101125010915/http://www.nfl.com/news/story/09000d5d81c535bf/article/childress-fired-by-vikings-frazier-named-interim-head-coach |url-status=live }}</ref> was the team's head coach from January 3, 2011, until December 30, 2013, when he was fired after compiling a {{win–loss record|w=21|l=32|t=1}} record as head coach.<ref>{{cite news |first=Gregg |last=Rosenthal |title=Leslie Frazier has been fired by Minnesota Vikings |url=https://www.nfl.com/news/leslie-frazier-fired-as-minnesota-vikings-coach-0ap2000000306942 |website=NFL.com |publisher=[[National Football League]] |date=December 30, 2013 |access-date=December 30, 2013 |archive-date=December 30, 2013 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20131230233906/http://www.nfl.com/news/story/0ap2000000306942/article/leslie-frazier-has-been-fired-by-minnesota-vikings |url-status=live }}</ref> On January 15, 2014, the Vikings appointed [[Mike Zimmer]] as the team's ninth head coach.<ref>{{cite magazine |first=Doug |last=Farrar |title=Mike Zimmer hired as coach of Vikings |url=http://nfl.si.com/2014/01/15/mike-zimmer-hired-minnesota-vikings-head-coach/ |magazine=[[Sports Illustrated]] |date=January 15, 2014 |access-date=January 15, 2014 |archive-date=January 16, 2014 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20140116051058/http://nfl.si.com/2014/01/15/mike-zimmer-hired-minnesota-vikings-head-coach/ |url-status=live }}</ref> He served for eight years until being fired on January 10, 2022, compiling a {{win–loss record|w=72|l=56|t=1}} record with the team.<ref>{{Cite web |date=January 10, 2022 |title=Vikings fire head coach Mike Zimmer, GM Rick Spielman |url=https://www.nfl.com/news/vikings-fire-hc-mike-zimmer-gm-rick-spielman |access-date=December 19, 2023 |website=NFL.com |publisher=[[National Football League]] |archive-date=January 10, 2022 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220110142032/https://www.nfl.com/news/vikings-fire-hc-mike-zimmer-gm-rick-spielman |url-status=live }}</ref> Two days after he helped the [[Los Angeles Rams]] to victory in [[Super Bowl LVI]] as their offensive coordinator, the Vikings signed [[Kevin O'Connell (American football)|Kevin O'Connell]] to be their 10th head coach in team history.

==History==
Following the Minnesota Vikings' admission to the [[National Football League]], there were ultimately two candidates for the position of head coach: [[Philadelphia Eagles]] quarterback [[Norm Van Brocklin]] and [[Winnipeg Blue Bombers]] head coach [[Bud Grant]]. Van Brocklin was favored by three of the Vikings' five board members, and after discussions with the franchise management on January 18, Van Brocklin signed an initial three-year contract and was appointed as head coach on January 18, 1961.<ref>{{cite news |title=Van Brocklin or Grant? Minnesota Owners Divided Over Choice of Head Coach |url=https://news.google.com/newspapers?id=9nURAAAAIBAJ&sjid=0-IDAAAAIBAJ&pg=2912,2692381 |work=[[The Register-Guard|Eugene Register-Guard]] |publisher=Guard Publishing |date=January 18, 1961 |access-date=November 26, 2009 }}</ref><ref>{{cite news |title=Norm Van Brocklin Begins Career as Vikings Coach |url=https://news.google.com/newspapers?id=A_dNAAAAIBAJ&sjid=IYsDAAAAIBAJ&pg=5030,1044425 |work=[[The Free Lance–Star]] |publisher=Free Lance–Star Publishing Company |date=January 19, 1961 |access-date=January 12, 2011 |archive-date=March 23, 2017 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170323151445/https://news.google.com/newspapers?id=A_dNAAAAIBAJ&sjid=IYsDAAAAIBAJ&pg=5030,1044425 |url-status=live }}</ref> In Van Brocklin's first season in charge of the Vikings, the team won just three of their 14 games, a record that got worse before it got better. The team had a record of {{win–loss record|w=2|l=11|d=1}} in Van Brocklin's second season as head coach, but improved to {{win–loss record|w=8|l=5|t=1}} in the [[1964 Minnesota Vikings season|1964 season]].<ref name="Van Brocklin"/> However, this was not enough to reach the NFL Championship Game as the team finished tied for second place in the Western Conference.<ref>{{cite web |title=1964 Minnesota Vikings Statistics & Players |url=https://www.pro-football-reference.com/teams/min/1964.htm |website=[[Pro-Football-Reference.com]] |publisher=[[Sports Reference]] |access-date=November 26, 2009 |archive-date=July 25, 2012 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120725135854/http://www.pro-football-reference.com/teams/min/1964.htm |url-status=live }}</ref>

By Van Brocklin's final season at the helm, his relationship with starting quarterback [[Fran Tarkenton]] had deteriorated to the point that the two could no longer work together. This resulted in Van Brocklin's resignation on February 11, 1967, shortly followed by Tarkenton being traded to the [[New York Giants]].<ref>{{cite news |title=Van Cites Tarkenton Tiff |url=https://news.google.com/newspapers?id=zigRAAAAIBAJ&sjid=vugDAAAAIBAJ&pg=7448,4200686 |work=[[The Spokesman-Review]] |publisher=[[Cowles Publishing Company]] |date=February 13, 1967 |access-date=November 26, 2009 |archive-date=March 23, 2017 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170323150709/https://news.google.com/newspapers?id=zigRAAAAIBAJ&sjid=vugDAAAAIBAJ&pg=7448,4200686 |url-status=live }}</ref><ref>{{cite news |title=Tarkenton Traded to New York Club |url=https://news.google.com/newspapers?id=oKoSAAAAIBAJ&sjid=xfcDAAAAIBAJ&pg=6838,1843290 |work=[[Spokane Daily Chronicle]] |date=March 7, 1967 |access-date=November 26, 2009 |archive-date=March 23, 2017 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170323180232/https://news.google.com/newspapers?id=oKoSAAAAIBAJ&sjid=xfcDAAAAIBAJ&pg=6838,1843290 |url-status=live }}</ref> In the search for Van Brocklin's replacement, Vikings founder [[Max Winter]] and general manager [[Jim Finks]] re-approached Bud Grant, who joined the Minnesota side on March 10, 1967, after 10 seasons coaching the Winnipeg Blue Bombers.<ref>{{Cite news |date=March 13, 1967 |title=Grant takes over Minnesota reins |work=[[The Bulletin (Bend)|The Bulletin]] |publisher=[[Western Communications]] |url=https://news.google.com/newspapers?id=CJUSAAAAIBAJ&sjid=3_YDAAAAIBAJ&pg=5455,1872511 |access-date=November 26, 2009 |archive-date=March 23, 2017 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170323171200/https://news.google.com/newspapers?id=CJUSAAAAIBAJ&sjid=3_YDAAAAIBAJ&pg=5455,1872511 |url-status=live }}</ref> With a record of {{win–loss record|w=8|l=6}}, the Vikings finished top of their division in Grant's second season in charge, reaching the playoffs for the first time in franchise history. However, they lost out to the [[History of the Baltimore Colts|Baltimore Colts]] 24–14 in their Western Conference Championship Game.<ref>{{cite web |title=1968 NFL Standings, Team & Offensive Statistics |url=https://www.pro-football-reference.com/years/1968/ |website=[[Pro-Football-Reference.com]] |publisher=[[Sports Reference]] |access-date=November 26, 2009 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20131115045559/http://www.pro-football-reference.com/years/1968/ |archive-date=November 15, 2013 }}</ref> The following year, they went two better by beating the [[Los Angeles Rams]] and the [[Cleveland Browns]] to claim the NFL Championship, before losing out to the [[Kansas City Chiefs]] 23–7 in [[Super Bowl IV]].<ref>{{cite web |title=1969 NFL Standings, Team & Offensive Statistics |url=https://www.pro-football-reference.com/years/1969/ |website=[[Pro-Football-Reference.com]] |publisher=[[Sports Reference]] |access-date=November 26, 2009 |archive-date=April 22, 2009 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090422162455/http://www.pro-football-reference.com/years/1969/ |url-status=live }}</ref> Nine more divisional titles followed in the next 11 seasons, including NFC Championships in [[1973 Minnesota Vikings season|1973]], [[1974 Minnesota Vikings season|1974]] and [[1976 Minnesota Vikings season|1976]], making Grant the first head coach to lead a team to four Super Bowls, although he won none of them.

Grant retired as head coach after the [[1983 Minnesota Vikings season|1983 season]], and was replaced by receivers coach [[Les Steckel]] in January 1984.<ref>{{cite news |title=Steckel named Vikings' coach |url=https://news.google.com/newspapers?id=NE8NAAAAIBAJ&sjid=720DAAAAIBAJ&pg=6381,5726782 |work=[[Pittsburgh Post-Gazette]] |publisher=[[Block Communications]] |date=January 30, 1984 |access-date=November 26, 2009 |archive-date=March 23, 2017 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170323143757/https://news.google.com/newspapers?id=NE8NAAAAIBAJ&sjid=720DAAAAIBAJ&pg=6381,5726782 |url-status=live }}</ref> However, under Steckel, the team had their worst season since [[1962 Minnesota Vikings season|1962]], only managing to win three of their 16 games in [[1984 Minnesota Vikings season|1984]].<ref name="Steckel"/> After Steckel was fired, Grant was coaxed out of retirement to replace him for the [[1985 Minnesota Vikings season|1985 season]].<ref>{{cite news |title=Vikings turn to familiar face, restore Bud Grant as coach |url=https://news.google.com/newspapers?id=7hEOAAAAIBAJ&sjid=0XwDAAAAIBAJ&pg=3076,4179402 |work=[[St. Petersburg Times]] |publisher=[[Times Publishing Company]] |date=December 19, 1984 |access-date=November 27, 2009 }} {{Dead link|date=October 2010|bot=H3llBot}}</ref> After Grant's second retirement, Vikings assistant coach [[Jerry Burns]] was named as his successor.<ref>{{cite news |title=Grant retires again |url=https://news.google.com/newspapers?id=FlQNAAAAIBAJ&sjid=Qm4DAAAAIBAJ&pg=4702,1357594 |work=[[Pittsburgh Post-Gazette]] |publisher=[[Block Communications]] |date=January 7, 1986 |access-date=November 27, 2009 |archive-date=March 23, 2017 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170323152111/https://news.google.com/newspapers?id=FlQNAAAAIBAJ&sjid=Qm4DAAAAIBAJ&pg=4702,1357594 |url-status=live }}</ref> Burns' tenure as head coach lasted for six seasons, including three playoff appearances, one of which resulted in a loss to the [[Washington Commanders|Washington Redskins]] in the 1987 NFC Championship Game.<ref name="Burns"/><ref>{{cite web |title=Minnesota Vikings at Washington Redskins - January 17th, 1988 |url=https://www.pro-football-reference.com/boxscores/198801170was.htm |website=[[Pro-Football-Reference.com]] |publisher=[[Sports Reference]] |access-date=November 27, 2009 |archive-date=January 4, 2009 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090104230210/http://www.pro-football-reference.com/boxscores/198801170was.htm |url-status=live }}</ref>

Burns retired from coaching at the end of the [[1991 Minnesota Vikings season|1991 season]],<ref>{{Cite news |date=December 5, 1991 |title=Sports People: Pro Football; Vikings Confirm Report That Burns Will Retire |work=[[The New York Times]] |url=https://www.nytimes.com/1991/12/05/sports/sports-people-pro-football-vikings-confirm-report-that-burns-will-retire.html |url-access=subscription |access-date=November 27, 2009 |archive-date=May 25, 2015 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20150525223955/http://www.nytimes.com/1991/12/05/sports/sports-people-pro-football-vikings-confirm-report-that-burns-will-retire.html |url-status=live }}</ref> and the Vikings turned to [[Stanford Cardinal football|Stanford Cardinal]] head coach [[Dennis Green]] as his successor, making Green the first African American head coach in franchise history.<ref>{{Cite news |last=Thomas |first=Robert McG. Jr. |date=January 11, 1992 |title=Football; N.F.L. Gets 2d Black Head Coach |work=[[The New York Times]] |url=https://www.nytimes.com/1992/01/11/sports/football-nfl-gets-2d-black-head-coach.html |url-access=subscription |access-date=November 27, 2009 |archive-date=May 11, 2014 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20140511050242/http://www.nytimes.com/1992/01/11/sports/football-nfl-gets-2d-black-head-coach.html |url-status=live }}</ref> In the first nine years of Green's tenure with the Vikings, the closest he came to a losing record was an {{win–loss record|w=8|l=8}} record in [[1995 Minnesota Vikings season|1995]], the only season in which his team missed the playoffs.<ref name="Green"/> Three years later, Green's team played out the best season in franchise history, losing only to the [[Tampa Bay Buccaneers]] on the way to a {{win–loss record|w=15|l=1}} record.<ref name="1998_season">{{cite web |title=1998 Minnesota Vikings Statistics & Players |url=https://www.pro-football-reference.com/teams/min/1998.htm |website=[[Pro-Football-Reference.com]] |publisher=[[Sports Reference]] |access-date=November 27, 2009 |archive-date=February 9, 2010 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20100209032621/http://www.pro-football-reference.com/teams/min/1998.htm |url-status=live }}</ref> The team received a bye to the Divisional Playoffs, in which they beat the [[Arizona Cardinals]] to set up a Conference Championship Game against the [[Atlanta Falcons]].<ref name="1998_season"/> With six minutes left in the fourth quarter and the Vikings in the lead at 27–20, they drove down the field to set up a 38-yard field goal for kicker [[Gary Anderson (placekicker)|Gary Anderson]], who had not missed a single kick all season. A successful kick would have given the Vikings a two-score lead with just over two minutes left to play, but Anderson hooked his kick wide left, allowing the Falcons to take the ball back downfield for a game-tying touchdown. They followed this with a field goal in overtime, denying the Vikings a fifth Super Bowl appearance.<ref>{{cite web |title=Atlanta Falcons at Minnesota Vikings - January 17th, 1999 |url=https://www.pro-football-reference.com/boxscores/199901170min.htm |website=[[Pro-Football-Reference.com]] |publisher=[[Sports Reference]] |access-date=November 27, 2009 |archive-date=January 30, 2018 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180130013646/https://www.pro-football-reference.com/boxscores/199901170min.htm |url-status=live }}</ref>

Green's 10th season at the Vikings helm turned out to be his final year in Minnesota; with a {{win–loss record|w=5|l=10}} record with one game remaining in the [[2001 Minnesota Vikings season|2001 season]], the Vikings management bought out the final two years of Green's contract and promoted offensive line coach and former tight end [[Mike Tice]] to the top job for the final game of the season.<ref>{{cite news |title=Green, Vikings agree to buyout |url=https://www.espn.com/nfl/news/story?id=1305597 |work=[[ESPN]] |agency=[[Associated Press]] |date=January 5, 2002 |access-date=November 27, 2009 |archive-date=November 3, 2012 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20121103184513/http://sports.espn.go.com/nfl/news/story?id=1305597 |url-status=live }}</ref> Tice remained in the job for a further four seasons, but only reached the playoffs once, losing out to the Philadelphia Eagles in the Divisional Playoffs of the [[2004 Minnesota Vikings season|2004 season]].<ref name="Tice"/> Tice's contract was allowed to expire at the end of the [[2005 Minnesota Vikings season|2005 season]], and he was quickly replaced by Eagles offensive coordinator [[Brad Childress]].<ref>{{cite news |title=Vikings name Childress their new coach |url=https://www.usatoday.com/sports/football/nfl/vikings/2006-01-06-childress-coach_x.htm |work=[[USA Today]] |date=January 6, 2006 |access-date=November 26, 2009 |archive-date=January 12, 2006 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20060112183349/http://www.usatoday.com/sports/football/nfl/vikings/2006-01-06-childress-coach_x.htm |url-status=live }}</ref> After Childress' first season in charge, the Vikings' regular season record improved by two wins a season from {{win–loss record|w=6|l=10}} in [[2006 Minnesota Vikings season|2006]] to {{win–loss record|w=12|l=4}} in [[2009 Minnesota Vikings season|2009]]. They reached the playoffs as NFC North champions in consecutive years in 2008 and 2009; they lost out to Childress' former team, the Eagles, in the 2008 NFC Wildcard game, but beat the [[Dallas Cowboys]] in the 2009 Divisional game to reach their first NFC Championship Game since [[2000 Minnesota Vikings season|2000]]. However, they lost to the [[New Orleans Saints]] and missed out on the Super Bowl.<ref name="Childress"/> The following season, the Vikings picked up just three wins in their first 10 games; after the seventh defeat of the season, Childress was fired and defensive coordinator [[Leslie Frazier]] took over as interim head coach.<ref name="childress_fired"/> Frazier was named head coach on a permanent basis on January 3, 2011, but his first full season in charge saw the Vikings finish with a {{win–loss record|w=3|l=13}} record. The following year saw a dramatic turnaround as the Vikings finished at {{win–loss record|w=10|l=6}} in [[2012 Minnesota Vikings season|2012]], just edging out the [[Chicago Bears]] to make the playoffs as the NFC's sixth seed with a win over the [[Green Bay Packers]] in week 17; however, the team slumped again in [[2013 Minnesota Vikings season|2013]], as a final {{win–loss record|w=5|l=10|t=1}} record ultimately saw Frazier fired on December 30, 2013.<ref>{{cite news |title=Vikings fire Leslie Frazier |url=https://www.espn.com/nfl/story/_/id/10214415/minnesota-vikings-announce-leslie-frazier-head-coach |work=[[ESPN]] |date=December 30, 2013 |access-date=December 30, 2013 |archive-date=December 30, 2013 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20131230235002/http://espn.go.com/nfl/story/_/id/10214415/minnesota-vikings-announce-leslie-frazier-head-coach |url-status=live }}</ref>

On January 15, 2014, the Vikings announced the hiring of the [[Cincinnati Bengals]]' defensive coordinator [[Mike Zimmer]] as head coach, and in his first year at the helm, the team finished at {{win–loss record|w=7|l=9}}, just missing the playoffs. The Vikings again improved to {{win–loss record|w=11|l=5}} in 2015, beating the Packers in week 17 to win the [[NFC North]] for the first time since 2009 and snapping a streak of five consecutive titles by Green Bay; however, they went on to lose to the Seattle Seahawks in the wildcard round of the playoffs. After going {{win–loss record|w=5|l=0}} to start the [[2016 Minnesota Vikings season|2016 season]] (which was their first season in their newly completed [[U.S. Bank Stadium]]) despite a slew of injuries, the team won just three games after their bye week and finished {{win–loss record|w=8|l=8}}. In 2017, Zimmer led the team to a {{win–loss record|w=13|l=3}} record and a first-round bye on the way to the NFC Championship Game against the [[2017 Philadelphia Eagles season|Eagles]], who won 38–7, leaving the Vikings just short of becoming the first team ever to reach a Super Bowl played in their own stadium. Zimmer's Vikings posted back-to-back losing seasons in 2020 and 2021, and on January 10, 2022, he was fired with a record of just three playoff appearances and two division titles in his eight seasons with the team.<ref>{{Cite magazine |last=Ragatz |first=Will |date=January 10, 2022 |title=Vikings Fire Head Coach Mike Zimmer After Eight Seasons and Three Playoff Appearances |url=https://www.si.com/nfl/vikings/news/vikings-fire-head-coach-mike-zimmer-eight-seasons-three-playoff-appearances |magazine=[[Sports Illustrated]] |access-date=January 10, 2022 |archive-date=January 10, 2022 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220110142131/https://www.si.com/nfl/vikings/news/vikings-fire-head-coach-mike-zimmer-eight-seasons-three-playoff-appearances |url-status=live }}</ref> Los Angeles Rams offensive coordinator [[Kevin O'Connell (American football)|Kevin O'Connell]] was appointed as Zimmer's replacement in February 2022, shortly after helping the Rams to victory in [[Super Bowl LVI]].<ref>{{Cite web|date=February 16, 2022|title=Vikings announce Kevin O'Connell as next head coach|url=https://profootballtalk.nbcsports.com/2022/02/16/vikings-announce-kevin-oconnell-as-next-head-coach/|access-date=February 16, 2022|website=[[ProFootballTalk]]|archive-date=February 16, 2022|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220216170810/https://profootballtalk.nbcsports.com/2022/02/16/vikings-announce-kevin-oconnell-as-next-head-coach/|url-status=live}}</ref>

Detroit Lions:
{{Short description|None}} {{featured list}} {{Use mdy dates|date=June 2024}} [[File:Rivera-Campbell-shake (cropped).jpg|thumb|[[Dan Campbell]] is the current head coach of the Detroit Lions]] The [[Detroit Lions]] are a professional American football team based in [[Detroit]], Michigan. They are currently a member of the [[NFC North|North Division]] of the [[National Football Conference]] (NFC) in the [[National Football League]] (NFL). The franchise has had 30 head coaches in team history, which includes its existence as the Portsmouth (Ohio) Spartans (1930–1933). In the [[1934 NFL season]], the franchise moved to Detroit and changed their name to the Lions. [[George Clark (American football coach)|George "Potsy" Clark]] is the only coach to have more than one tenure. Three coaches have won [[History of National Football League Championship|NFL championships]] with the team: Potsy Clark in [[NFL Championship Game, 1935|1935]], [[Buddy Parker]] in [[NFL Championship Game, 1952|1952]] and [[NFL Championship Game, 1953|1953]], and [[George Wilson (football coach)|George Wilson]] in [[NFL Championship Game, 1957|1957]].{{Cite web |title=Detroit Lions Championship History |url=http://www.nflteamhistory.com/nfl_teams/detroit_lions/championship_history.html |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20080527081115/http://www.nflteamhistory.com/nfl_teams/detroit_lions/championship_history.html |archive-date=May 27, 2008 |access-date=March 19, 2008 |website=NFLTeamHistory.com}} [[Wayne Fontes]] is the all-time leader in games coached and wins, and Clark leads all coaches in winning percentage with .679 (with at least one full season coached). [[John Karcis]] is statistically the worst coach the Lions have had as he never won a game.{{Cite web |title=Marty Mornhinweg's Coaching Record |url=https://www.pro-football-reference.com/coaches/MornMa0.htm |access-date=March 20, 2008 |publisher=Pro Football Reference |archive-date=December 20, 2007 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20071220021913/http://www.pro-football-reference.com/coaches/MornMa0.htm |url-status=live }} Karcis is followed by [[Marty Mornhinweg]] with a winning percentage of .156. Of the 30 Lions coaches, two have been elected into the [[Pro Football Hall of Fame]]: [[Dutch Clark]] and [[Joe Schmidt (American football)|Joe Schmidt]].{{Cite web |title=Hall of Famers by Franchise |url=http://www.profootballhof.com/hof/teams.aspx |access-date=March 19, 2008 |website=[[Pro Football Hall of Fame]] |archive-date=October 15, 2015 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20151015213446/http://www.profootballhof.com/hof/teams.aspx |url-status=live }} [[Gus Dorais]] was inducted in the [[College Football Hall of Fame]] as a coach in 1954. Several former players have been head coach for the Lions, including Dutch Edwards, Buddy Parker, [[Harry Gilmer]], Joe Schmidt, [[Dick Jauron]], and the current head coach [[Dan Campbell]], who was hired on January 20, 2021.{{Cite web |date=January 20, 2021 |title=Lions agree to terms with Dan Campbell to become team's new head coach |url=https://www.detroitlions.com/news/lions-agree-to-terms-with-dan-campbell-to-become-team-s-new-head-coach |access-date=January 20, 2021 |website=detroitlions.com |archive-date=February 27, 2021 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210227201222/https://www.detroitlions.com/news/lions-agree-to-terms-with-dan-campbell-to-become-team-s-new-head-coach |url-status=live }} == Coaches == ''Note: Statistics are accurate through Week 18 of the [[2025 NFL season]].'' {| class="wikitable" style="font-size:100%;" |'''GC''' |Games coached |- |'''W''' |Wins |- |'''L''' |Losses |- |'''T''' |Ties |- |'''Win%''' |Win – Loss percentage |- |'''Div''' |Division titles |- | style="background: #FFE6BD;" |* |Elected to the [[Pro Football Hall of Fame]] |- | style="background: #DDFFDD;" |† |Spent entire professional head coaching career with the Lions |} {| class="wikitable" width=75% ! rowspan="2" | # ! rowspan="2" | Image ! rowspan="2" | Name ! rowspan="2" | Term ! colspan="5" | Regular season ! rowspan="2" | Division

Tampa Bay Buccaneers:
{{Short description|Buccaneers}} {{Use mdy dates|date=June 2024}} [[Image:Jon Gruden2.jpg|300px|thumb|right|Jon Gruden was the Buccaneers' head coach between 2002 and 2008.]] The [[Tampa Bay Buccaneers]] are a professional [[American football]] team based in [[Tampa, Florida|Tampa]], [[Florida]]. They are members of the [[NFC South|Southern Division]] of the [[National Football Conference]] (NFC) in the [[National Football League]] (NFL). The franchise was founded as an NFL team in 1976 by [[Hugh Culverhouse]]. They lost their first 26 games and had one playoff win in its first 21 seasons before winning the [[Super Bowl]] in [[Super Bowl XXXVII|2002]].{{cite web|access-date=December 8, 2008|url=http://www.sportsline.com/nfl/teams/history/TB|title=Tampa Bay Buccaneers |work=CBS Sportsline }} There have been twelve head coaches for the Buccaneers franchise. The team has played 628 games in 50 seasons since joining the NFL. Five Buccaneers coaches, [[John McKay (football coach)|John McKay]], [[Tony Dungy]], [[Jon Gruden]], [[Bruce Arians]], and [[Todd Bowles]] have taken the Buccaneers to the playoffs, with Gruden and Arians being the only two coaches to win the [[Super Bowl]] with the team, at [[Super Bowl XXXVII]] and [[Super Bowl LV]] respectively.{{cite web | publisher=NFL Team History | url=http://www.nflteamhistory.com/nfl_teams/tampa_bay_buccaneers/championship_history.html | title=Tampa Bay Buccaneers Championship History | access-date=March 25, 2008}} The team's all-time leader in games coached is McKay (133) and the leader in wins is Gruden (57); Arians leads all Buccaneers coaches in [[winning percentage]] (.633).{{cite web|url=https://www.pro-football-reference.com/coaches/AriaBr0.htm|title=Bruce Arians' Coaching Record|work=Pro Football Reference |publisher=Sports Reference LLC|access-date=March 25, 2008}} [[Leeman Bennett]] has the lowest winning percentage (.125) of all Buccaneers coaches.{{cite web|url=https://www.pro-football-reference.com/coaches/BennLe0.htm|title=Leeman Bennett's Coaching Record|work=Pro Football Reference |publisher=Sports Reference LLC|access-date=March 25, 2008}} ==Key== {| class="wikitable" |- |'''#''' |Number of coachesA running total of the number of coaches of the Buccaneers. Atlanta Falcons:
{{Short description|None}} {{Use American English|date=November 2025}} {{Use mdy dates|date=June 2024}} The [[Atlanta Falcons]] are an [[American football]] team based in [[Atlanta, Georgia]]. They are members of the South division of the [[National Football Conference]] (NFC) in the [[National Football League]] (NFL). The Falcons joined the NFL as an [[expansion team]] in 1966, and have compiled an all-time record of 337 wins, 436 losses, and 6 ties. The team has won the NFC West championship twice in [[1980 Atlanta Falcons season|1980]] and [[1998 Atlanta Falcons season|1998]] and the NFC South championship 4 times in [[2004 Atlanta Falcons season|2004]], [[2010 Atlanta Falcons season|2010]], [[2012 Atlanta Falcons season|2012]] and [[2016 Atlanta Falcons season|2016]]. The Falcons have appeared in two [[Super Bowl]]s, losing both times; their first appearance was in [[Super Bowl XXXIII]], with the Falcons falling to the [[1998 Denver Broncos season|Denver Broncos]] 19–34, and the second was in [[Super Bowl LI]], where the Falcons fell to the [[2016 New England Patriots season|New England Patriots]] 28–34 in overtime.{{cite web |url=http://www.profootballhof.com/history/team.jsp?franchise_id=2 |title=Atlanta Falcons |access-date=May 3, 2008 |work=Pro Football Hall of Fame |archive-date=January 5, 2008 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20080105024348/http://www.profootballhof.com/history/team.jsp?franchise_id=2 |url-status=live }} There have been 19 [[head coach]]es for the Falcons franchise, 14 serving full-time. Among full-time head coaches, [[Mike Smith (American football coach)|Mike Smith]] is the Falcons' winningest and longest tenured head coach, with a 66–46 regular season record. Under Smith's leadership, the team attained consecutive winning seasons (11–5 in 2008 and 9–7 in 2009), consecutive playoff appearances (2010 and 2011), and consecutive seasons with 10 wins or more (also in 2010 and 2011) for the first time in franchise history. Also, Smith is the only Falcons coach to win 2 divisional titles (NFC South, 2010 and 2012). The current head coach of the Falcons is [[Kevin Stefanski]].{{Cite web|url=https://www.atlantafalcons.com/news/atlanta-falcons-name-kevin-stefanski-head-coach|title=Atlanta Falcons name Kevin Stefanski head coach|publisher=[[Atlanta Falcons]]|date=January 17, 2026|accessdate=January 17, 2026}} ==Key== {| class="wikitable" |- |'''#''' |Number of coachesA running total of the number of coaches of the Falcons.
